nodes.label: Let $\epsilon >0$. Is it true that, if $k$ is sufficiently large, then\[R(G)>(1-\epsilon)^kR(k)\]for every graph $G$ with chromatic
nodes.statement: Let $\epsilon >0$. Is it true that, if $k$ is sufficiently large, then\[R(G)>(1-\epsilon)^kR(k)\]for every graph $G$ with chromatic number $\chi(G)=k$? Even stronger, is there some $c>0$ such that, for all large $k$, $R(G)>cR(k)$ for every graph $G$ with chromatic number $\chi(G)=k$?

nodes.label: For any $\epsilon>0$ there exists $\delta=\delta(\epsilon)>0$ such that if $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices with no independent set or
nodes.statement: For any $\epsilon>0$ there exists $\delta=\delta(\epsilon)>0$ such that if $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices with no independent set or clique of size $\geq \epsilon\log n$ then $G$ contains an induced subgraph with $m$ edges for all $m\leq \delta n^2$.

nodes.label: Does every set of $n$ distinct points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ determine $\gg n/\sqrt{\log n}$ many distinct distances?
nodes.statement: Does every set of $n$ distinct points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ determine $\gg n/\sqrt{\log n}$ many distinct distances?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_89 :
    (fun (n : ℕ) => n/(n : ℝ).log.sqrt) =O[atTop] (fun n => (minimalDistinctDistances n : ℝ))
nodes.closure_condition: Improve the Guth–Katz incidence/polynomial-partitioning lower bound from n/log n to the conjectured optimal n/√(log n) for the minimum number of distinct distances determined by n points in R^2. Concretely: close the √(log n) gap between the Guth–Katz bound (≫ n/log n distinct distances, proven via polynomial method + Elekes–Sharir line-incidence framework) and the √n×√n integer-grid upper bound (≍ n/√(log n) distinct distances, the conjectured extremal configuration whose count is governed by the Landau prime-of-form-x²+y² density). The missing object is a sharpened incidence estimate (or a different structural argument) eliminating the logarithmic loss in the Guth–Katz line-counting step. Note: even the stronger pin/averaged form (single point determines ≫ n/√(log n) distances; Σ_x d(x) ≫ n²/√(log n), Erdős [Er75f]) remains open.

nodes.label: Does every set of $n$ distinct points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ contain at most $n^{1+O(1/\log\log n)}$ many pairs which are distance 1 apart?
nodes.statement: Does every set of $n$ distinct points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ contain at most $n^{1+O(1/\log\log n)}$ many pairs which are distance 1 apart?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem unitDistanceCounts_BddAbove (n : ℕ) : BddAbove <| unitDistanceCounts n
nodes.closure_condition: SOLVED (disproved). The Erdős unit-distance conjecture — that every n-point set in R^2 determines at most n^{1+O(1/loglog n)} unit-distance pairs — is FALSE. Closed by an explicit construction (OpenAI internal model, see openai.com unit-distance-proof.pdf, ref [1085]): for infinitely many n, a point set P of n points in R^2 with at least n^{1+c} unit-distance pairs for an absolute constant c>0, violating the conjectured upper bound. The Lean formalization (erdos_90) records answer(False), category `research solved`. Note the broader open problem — the true growth rate / exact exponent of the maximum number of unit distances u(n), still gapped between ~n^{4/3} (Spencer–Szemerédi–Trotter upper bound) and n^{1+c/loglog n} (Erdős lower bound) — remains OPEN, but problem #90 as stated is settled in the negative.

nodes.label: Let $n$ be a sufficiently large integer. Suppose $A\subset \mathbb{R}^2$ has $\lvert A\rvert=n$ and minimises the number of distinct distanc
nodes.statement: Let $n$ be a sufficiently large integer. Suppose $A\subset \mathbb{R}^2$ has $\lvert A\rvert=n$ and minimises the number of distinct distances between points in $A$. Prove that there are at least two (and probably many) such $A$ which are non-similar.
nodes.formal_statement: lemma erdos_91.test.equiTriangle_optimal : IsOptimal equiTriangle 3
nodes.closure_condition: Need a construction/existence lemma producing, for every sufficiently large n, at least two non-similar n-point planar sets that each attain minimalDistinctDistances(n) (the minimum number of distinct distances). The bottleneck is exhibiting two non-similar OPTIMAL configurations simultaneously for all large n — equivalently, ruling out that the distinct-distance minimizer is unique-up-to-similarity. Note: even the exact value of minimalDistinctDistances(n) is itself open (problem #89), so this requires either characterizing the minimizers or a similarity-breaking argument that does not depend on knowing the exact minimum. NOT finite-closeable: small cases n=3..9 are already settled (n=3,5 unique; n=4,6,7,8,9 non-unique), so no finite witness resolves the 'sufficiently large n' claim.

nodes.label: Let $f(n)$ be maximal such that there exists a set $A$ of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ in which every $x\in A$ has at least $f(n)$ points in
nodes.statement: Let $f(n)$ be maximal such that there exists a set $A$ of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ in which every $x\in A$ has at least $f(n)$ points in $A$ equidistant from $x$.Is it true that $f(n)\leq n^{o(1)}$? Or even $f(n) < n^{O(1/\log\log n)}$?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem possible_f_values_BddAbove (n : ℕ) : BddAbove (possible_f_values n)
nodes.closure_condition: SOLVED (disproved in the negative). The conjecture f(n) <= n^{o(1)} is FALSE. Closure came via the disproof of the unit distance conjecture (#90): problem 92 is a strictly stronger form of the unit-distance conjecture, so the recent disproof of #90 transfers to disprove the n^{o(1)} bound here. Lattice points already give the matching lower bound f(n) > n^{c/loglog n}; current upper bounds f(n) << n^{4/11} (Hunter, via Janzer-Janzer-Methuku-Tardos circle-point incidence bound), improving Pach-Sharir n^{2/5} and trivial n^{1/2}. The single missing input that closed it = the impossibility certificate for the unit-distance n^{o(1)} bound (#90 disproof).

nodes.label: If $n$ distinct points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ form a convex polygon then they determine at least $\lfloor \frac{n}{2}\rfloor$ distinct distances.
nodes.statement: If $n$ distinct points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ form a convex polygon then they determine at least $\lfloor \frac{n}{2}\rfloor$ distinct distances.

nodes.label: Suppose $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ determine a convex polygon and the set of distances between them is $\{u_1,\ldots,u_t\}$. Suppose $u_i$
nodes.statement: Suppose $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ determine a convex polygon and the set of distances between them is $\{u_1,\ldots,u_t\}$. Suppose $u_i$ appears as the distance between $f(u_i)$ many pairs of points. Then\[\sum_i f(u_i)^2 \ll n^3.\]

nodes.label: Let $x_1,\ldots,x_n\in\mathbb{R}^2$ determine the set of distances $\{u_1,\ldots,u_t\}$. Suppose $u_i$ appears as the distance between $f(u_
nodes.statement: Let $x_1,\ldots,x_n\in\mathbb{R}^2$ determine the set of distances $\{u_1,\ldots,u_t\}$. Suppose $u_i$ appears as the distance between $f(u_i)$ many pairs of points. Then for all $\epsilon>0$\[\sum_i f(u_i)^2 \ll_\epsilon n^{3+\epsilon}.\]

nodes.label: If $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ form a convex polygon then there are $O(n)$ many pairs which are distance $1$ apart.
nodes.statement: If $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ form a convex polygon then there are $O(n)$ many pairs which are distance $1$ apart.
nodes.formal_statement: theorem convexUnitDistanceCounts_bddAbove (n : ℕ) : BddAbove <| convexUnitDistanceCounts n
nodes.closure_condition: A linear upper bound U_c(n) = O(n) on the maximum number of unit distances among the vertices of a convex n-gon, i.e. an incidence/combinatorial-geometry argument that removes the log factor from the current best bound n*log_2(n)+4n (Aggarwal 2015) and closes the gap to the Edelsbrunner-Hajnal 2n-7 lower bound (Erdos-Fishburn conjecture sharp constant 2n). Equivalently, prove the stronger sum_{x in A} g(x) < 4n bound, or close Erdos problem 97 from which a positive answer would follow.

nodes.label: Does every convex polygon have a vertex with no other $4$ vertices equidistant from it?
nodes.statement: Does every convex polygon have a vertex with no other $4$ vertices equidistant from it?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_97 :
    answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ A : Finset ℝ², A.Nonempty → ConvexIndep A → ¬HasNEquidistantProperty 4 A
nodes.closure_condition: A single finite convex polygon (a convex-independent point set A in R^2) in which every vertex has at least 4 OTHER vertices equidistant from it (the common radius may differ per vertex) — exhibiting such a configuration disproves the conjecture; proving no such polygon exists confirms it. The bottleneck object is the k=4 analogue of Danzer's 9-point / Fishburn-Reeds 20-point k=3 constructions: a convex polygon realizing HasNEquidistantProperty 4. (Open whether ANY such polygon exists for k=4; separately whether SOME finite constant k makes every convex polygon have a vertex lacking k equidistant vertices.)

nodes.label: Let $h(n)$ be such that any $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, with no three on a line and no four on a circle, determine at least $h(n)$ distinc
nodes.statement: Let $h(n)$ be such that any $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, with no three on a line and no four on a circle, determine at least $h(n)$ distinct distances. Does $h(n)/n\to \infty$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_98 :
    answer(sorry) ↔ Tendsto (fun n : ℕ ↦ ((h n : ℝ) / (n : ℝ))) atTop atTop
nodes.closure_condition: A super-linear lower bound on h(n), the minimum number of distinct distances determined by n points in the plane in general position (no 3 collinear, no 4 cocyclic), of the form h(n) >= n*w(n) with w(n) -> infinity. The bottleneck is purely the lower bound: even h(n) >= n is unproven (Erdos could only establish h(n) >= c*n trivially below n, while the general-position constraint is conjectured to force more distances than the unrestricted Erdos distinct-distances problem). Known upper bound h(n) < n*exp(c*sqrt(log n)) (Erdos-Furedi-Pach) shows the truth, if h(n)/n -> infinity, grows slower than any power of n, so the missing input is an incidence/general-position lower-bound argument certifying unbounded growth of h(n)/n. Not finitely closeable: an asymptotic statement about the n -> infinity limit.

nodes.label: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2$ be a set of $n$ points with minimum distance equal to 1, chosen to minimise the diameter of $A$. If $n$ is suff
nodes.statement: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2$ be a set of $n$ points with minimum distance equal to 1, chosen to minimise the diameter of $A$. If $n$ is sufficiently large then must there be three points in $A$ which form an equilateral triangle of size 1?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_99 :
    answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ᶠ n in Filter.atTop, ∀ A : Finset ℝ²,
      A.card = n → HasMinDist1 A →
      (IsMinOn (fun B: Finset ℝ² => diam (B : Set ℝ²)) {B : Finset ℝ² | B.card = n ∧ HasMinDist1 B} A) →
      ∃ᵉ (p ∈ A) (q ∈ A) (r ∈ A), FormsEquilateralTriangle p q r
nodes.closure_condition: Prove (or disprove) that for all sufficiently large n, every diameter-minimizing planar n-point set with minimum distance 1 contains a unit equilateral triangle. The precise bottleneck is a structural rigidity lemma forcing optimal configurations to coincide with the triangular lattice: specifically, show that any diameter-minimizer must have (1-o(1))n points lying on the triangular (hexagonal) lattice intersected with a disc — Erdős's stronger conjecture — which would force a unit equilateral triangle. Equivalently, a counterexample is an infinite family of diameter-optimal n-point min-distance-1 sets containing NO unit equilateral triangle. Owner notes Sendov and Simonovits doubted the conjecture, so direction itself is open. Statement is false for small n (n=4 square). Explicitly NOT finitely decidable: an 'eventually for large n' statement over a continuous configuration space.

nodes.label: Let $A$ be a set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that all pairwise distances are at least $1$ and if two distinct distances differ then
nodes.statement: Let $A$ be a set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that all pairwise distances are at least $1$ and if two distinct distances differ then they differ by at least $1$. Is the diameter of $A$ $\gg n$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_100 :
    answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ C > (0 : ℝ), ∀ᶠ n in atTop, ∀ A : Finset ℝ²,
      A.card = n →
      DistancesSeparated A →
      diam (A : Set ℝ²) > C * n
nodes.closure_condition: Remove the log-factor in the diameter lower bound: prove diam(A) >> n (linear) for any n-point planar set with pairwise distances >= 1 and distinct distances separated by >= 1. The bottleneck is improving the current best bound diam >> n/log n (inherited from the Guth-Katz distinct-distances theorem, which gives ~ n/log n distinct distances) up to the conjectured linear bound diam >= n - 1. Closing requires either a distinct-distances lower bound free of the log loss in this separated-distance regime, or a direct geometric argument exploiting the unit-separation of distinct distance values.

nodes.label: Given $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, no five of which are on a line, the number of lines containing four points is $o(n^2)$.
nodes.statement: Given $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, no five of which are on a line, the number of lines containing four points is $o(n^2)$.
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_101 : (fun n => (numLinesWithFourPointMax n : ℝ)) =o[atTop] (fun n => (n : ℝ)^2)
nodes.closure_condition: A proof of the upper bound: for any set of n points in R^2 with no 5 collinear, the number of lines containing exactly 4 points is o(n^2). The bottleneck is an upper-bound argument forcing subquadratic 4-rich-line count from the no-5-collinear hypothesis. The matching lower bound is essentially settled (Solymosi-Stojakovic [SoSt13] give n^{2-O(1/sqrt(log n))}, refuting Erdos's n^{3/2} guess), so what remains is purely the o(n^2) upper bound (ideally tightened toward n^{2-c}). Not finite-closeable: asymptotic-in-n, site notes it cannot be resolved by finite computation.

nodes.label: Let $c>0$ and $h_c(n)$ be such that for any $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that there are $\geq cn^2$ lines each containing more than
nodes.statement: Let $c>0$ and $h_c(n)$ be such that for any $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that there are $\geq cn^2$ lines each containing more than three points, there must be some line containing $h_c(n)$ many points. Estimate $h_c(n)$. Is it true that, for fixed $c>0$, we have $h_c(n)\to \infty$?

nodes.label: Let $h(n)$ count the number of incongruent sets of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ which minimise the diameter subject to the constraint that $
nodes.statement: Let $h(n)$ count the number of incongruent sets of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ which minimise the diameter subject to the constraint that $d(x,y)\geq 1$ for all points $x\neq y$. Is it true that $h(n)\to \infty$?

nodes.label: Given $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ the number of distinct unit circles containing at least three points is $o(n^2)$.
nodes.statement: Given $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ the number of distinct unit circles containing at least three points is $o(n^2)$.

nodes.label: Let $A,B\subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be disjoint sets of size $n$ and $n-3$ respectively, with not all of $A$ contained on a single line. Is there
nodes.statement: Let $A,B\subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be disjoint sets of size $n$ and $n-3$ respectively, with not all of $A$ contained on a single line. Is there a line which contains at least two points from $A$ and no points from $B$?

nodes.label: Draw $n$ squares inside the unit square with no common interior point. Let $f(n)$ be the maximum possible sum of the side-lengths of the squ
nodes.statement: Draw $n$ squares inside the unit square with no common interior point. Let $f(n)$ be the maximum possible sum of the side-lengths of the squares. Is $f(k^2+1)=k$?

nodes.label: Let $f(n)$ be minimal such that any $f(n)$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, no three on a line, contain $n$ points which form the vertices of a con
nodes.statement: Let $f(n)$ be minimal such that any $f(n)$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, no three on a line, contain $n$ points which form the vertices of a convex $n$-gon. Prove that $f(n)=2^{n-2}+1$.
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_107 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ n ≥ 3, f n = 2^(n - 2) + 1
nodes.closure_condition: Prove the matching upper bound f(n) <= 2^(n-2) + 1 for the Erdos-Szekeres function f(n) (minimal N so that any N points in general position contain a convex n-gon). The lower bound f(n) >= 2^(n-2)+1 is established (ErSz60 construction); the bottleneck is a tight upper bound. Current best (Holmsen-Mojarrad-Pach-Tardos 2020) is f(n) <= 2^(n+O(sqrt(n log n))), so the missing input is the sub-exponential-factor improvement closing 2^(n+o(n)) down to exactly 2^(n-2)+1.

nodes.label: For every $r\geq 4$ and $k\geq 2$ is there some finite $f(k,r)$ such that every graph of chromatic number $\geq f(k,r)$ contains a subgraph
nodes.statement: For every $r\geq 4$ and $k\geq 2$ is there some finite $f(k,r)$ such that every graph of chromatic number $\geq f(k,r)$ contains a subgraph of girth $\geq r$ and chromatic number $\geq k$?

references.label: the entry in the graphs problem collection

nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_108 :
    answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ r ≥ 4, ∀ k ≥ (2 : ℕ), ∃ (f : ℕ),
    ∀ (V : Type u) (G : SimpleGraph V) (_ : Nonempty V)
      (hchro : f ≤ SimpleGraph.chromaticNumber G),
    ∃ (H : G.Subgraph), (SimpleGraph.girth H.coe ≥ r) ∧
    (SimpleGraph.chromaticNumber H.coe ≥ k)
nodes.closure_condition: A function f(k,r) (finite for all r≥5, k≥3) such that every graph with chromatic number ≥ f(k,r) contains a subgraph of girth ≥ r and chromatic number ≥ k — i.e. a high-chromatic + high-girth subgraph extraction theorem for r≥5. The r=4 case is settled (Rödl [Ro77]); the missing input is the existence proof of this finite bound for arbitrary large girth r (Erdős–Hajnal conjecture), plus the open infinite-chromatic variant (#740). NOT finitely closeable: explicitly stated on the page as "cannot be resolved with a finite computation."

nodes.label: Any $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ of positive upper density contains a sumset $B+C$ where both $B$ and $C$ are infinite.
nodes.statement: Any $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ of positive upper density contains a sumset $B+C$ where both $B$ and $C$ are infinite.
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_109 (A : Set ℕ) (h : A.upperDensity > 0) :
    ∃ B C : Set ℕ, B.Infinite ∧ C.Infinite ∧ B + C ⊆ A
nodes.closure_condition: SOLVED. The Erdős sumset conjecture: every A ⊆ ℕ of positive upper Banach density contains a sumset B+C with both B and C infinite. Proved by Moreira, Richter, and Robertson, "A proof of a sumset conjecture of Erdős," Annals of Math. 189 (2019), 605-652 [MRR19], via ergodic-theoretic / measure-preserving-system methods (Furstenberg correspondence, dynamics of the orbit closure). The Lean statement (FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/109.lean) is tagged `research solved` but the proof body is `sorry`, so the only remaining input is a FULL FORMAL Lean proof of the MRR19 theorem (not a missing mathematical lemma — the mathematics is closed).

nodes.label: Is there some $F(n)$ such that every graph with chromatic number $\aleph_1$ has, for all large $n$, a subgraph with chromatic number $n$ on
nodes.statement: Is there some $F(n)$ such that every graph with chromatic number $\aleph_1$ has, for all large $n$, a subgraph with chromatic number $n$ on at most $F(n)$ vertices?

nodes.label: If $G$ is a graph let $h_G(n)$ be defined such that any subgraph of $G$ on $n$ vertices can be made bipartite after deleting at most $h_G(n)
nodes.statement: If $G$ is a graph let $h_G(n)$ be defined such that any subgraph of $G$ on $n$ vertices can be made bipartite after deleting at most $h_G(n)$ edges. What is the behaviour of $h_G(n)$? Is it true that $h_G(n)/n\to \infty$ for every graph $G$ with chromatic number $\aleph_1$?

nodes.label: Let $k=k(n,m)$ be minimal such that any directed graph on $k$ vertices must contain either an independent set of size $n$ or a transitive to
nodes.statement: Let $k=k(n,m)$ be minimal such that any directed graph on $k$ vertices must contain either an independent set of size $n$ or a transitive tournament of size $m$. Determine $k(n,m)$.

nodes.label: If $G$ is bipartite then $\mathrm{ex}(n;G)\ll n^{3/2}$ if and only $G$ is $2$-degenerate, that is, $G$ contains no induced subgraph with min
nodes.statement: If $G$ is bipartite then $\mathrm{ex}(n;G)\ll n^{3/2}$ if and only $G$ is $2$-degenerate, that is, $G$ contains no induced subgraph with minimal degree at least 3.

references.label: the entry in the graphs problem collection

nodes.label: If $p(z)\in\mathbb{C}[z]$ is a monic polynomial of degree $n$ then is the length of the curve $\{ z\in \mathbb{C} : \lvert p(z)\rvert=1\}$ m
nodes.statement: If $p(z)\in\mathbb{C}[z]$ is a monic polynomial of degree $n$ then is the length of the curve $\{ z\in \mathbb{C} : \lvert p(z)\rvert=1\}$ maximised when $p(z)=z^n-1$?

nodes.label: If $p(z)$ is a polynomial of degree $n$ such that $\{z : \lvert p(z)\rvert\leq 1\}$ is connected then is it true that\[\max_{\substack{z\in\
nodes.statement: If $p(z)$ is a polynomial of degree $n$ such that $\{z : \lvert p(z)\rvert\leq 1\}$ is connected then is it true that\[\max_{\substack{z\in\mathbb{C}\\ \lvert p(z)\rvert\leq 1}} \lvert p'(z)\rvert \leq (\tfrac{1}{2}+o(1))n^2?\]

nodes.label: Let $p(z)=\prod_{i=1}^n (z-z_i)$ for $\lvert z_i\rvert \leq 1$. Is it true that\[\lvert\{ z: \lvert p(z)\rvert <1\}\rvert>n^{-O(1)}\](or per
nodes.statement: Let $p(z)=\prod_{i=1}^n (z-z_i)$ for $\lvert z_i\rvert \leq 1$. Is it true that\[\lvert\{ z: \lvert p(z)\rvert <1\}\rvert>n^{-O(1)}\](or perhaps even $>(\log n)^{-O(1)}$)?

nodes.label: Let $h(n)$ be minimal such that any group $G$ with the property that any subset of $>n$ elements contains some $x\neq y$ such that $xy=y
nodes.statement: Let $h(n)$ be minimal such that any group $G$ with the property that any subset of $>n$ elements contains some $x\neq y$ such that $xy=yx$ can be covered by at most $h(n)$ many Abelian subgroups.Estimate $h(n)$ as well as possible.

nodes.label: Let $\alpha$ be a cardinal or ordinal number or an order type such that every two-colouring of $K_\alpha$ contains either a red $K_\alpha$ o
nodes.statement: Let $\alpha$ be a cardinal or ordinal number or an order type such that every two-colouring of $K_\alpha$ contains either a red $K_\alpha$ or a blue $K_3$. For every $n\geq 3$ must every two-colouring of $K_\alpha$ contain either a red $K_\alpha$ or a blue $K_n$?

nodes.label: Let $z_i$ be an infinite sequence of complex numbers such that $\lvert z_i\rvert=1$ for all $i\geq 1$, and for $n\geq 1$ let\[p_n(z)=\prod_{
nodes.statement: Let $z_i$ be an infinite sequence of complex numbers such that $\lvert z_i\rvert=1$ for all $i\geq 1$, and for $n\geq 1$ let\[p_n(z)=\prod_{i\leq n} (z-z_i).\]Let $M_n=\max_{\lvert z\rvert=1}\lvert p_n(z)\rvert$. Is it true that $\limsup M_n=\infty$?Is it true that there exists $c>0$ such that for infinitely many $n$ we have $M_n > n^c$?Is it true that there exists $c>0$ such that, for all large $n$,\[\sum_{k\leq n}M_k > n^{1+c}?\]
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_119.parts.i :
    answer(True) ↔ ∀ (z : ℕ → ℂ) (hz : ∀ i : ℕ, ‖z i‖ = 1),
      atTop.limsup (fun n => (M z n : EReal)) = ⊤
nodes.closure_condition: Prove or refute Part (iii): does there exist c>0 such that for all large n, the partial sum S_n = sum_{k<=n} M_k > n^{1+c}, where M_k = max_{|z|=1} |prod_{i<=k}(z - z_i)| for any sequence z_i on the unit circle. Parts (i) limsup M_n = infinity (Wagner [Wa80], even M_n > (log n)^c i.o.) and (ii) M_n > n^c i.o. in the max-window sense (Beck [Be91]: max_{n<=N} M_n > N^c) are SOLVED; the single remaining bottleneck is a lower bound on the AVERAGE growth of M_k forcing a super-linear (n^{1+c}) Cesaro sum. Known upper-side constraints: Erdos's construction gives M_n <= n+1 and Linden [Li77] gives a sequence with M_n << n^{1-c}, so the sought lower bound must hold despite sequences whose individual M_n stay below n.

nodes.label: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an infinite set. Must there be a set $E\subset \mathbb{R}$ of positive measure which does not contain any set
nodes.statement: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ be an infinite set. Must there be a set $E\subset \mathbb{R}$ of positive measure which does not contain any set of the shape $aA+b$ for some $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ and $a\neq 0$?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_120 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ A : Set ℝ, A.Infinite → Erdos120For A
nodes.closure_condition: Reduces to the single critical case: prove that the geometric sequence A = {1, 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, ...} is "universal" — i.e., disprove it by exhibiting a positive-measure set E ⊆ ℝ containing no affine copy aA+b (a≠0), OR prove no such E exists. The general infinite case reduces to A a strictly decreasing null sequence converging to 0 (unbounded/interval-dense A already done via Steinhaus density); the dyadic sequence {2^-n} (= Green's open-problems #94) is the open bottleneck instance. A finite witness E of positive measure avoiding all affine copies of {2^-n} would close that flagship case.

nodes.label: Let $F_{k}(N)$ be the size of the largest $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that the product of no $k$ many distinct elements of $A$ is a squ
nodes.statement: Let $F_{k}(N)$ be the size of the largest $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that the product of no $k$ many distinct elements of $A$ is a square. Is $F_5(N)=(1-o(1))N$? More generally, is $F_{2k+1}(N)=(1-o(1))N$?

nodes.label: For which number theoretic functions $f$ is it true that, for any $F(n)$ such that $F(n)/f(n)\to 0$ for almost all $n$, there are infinitely
nodes.statement: For which number theoretic functions $f$ is it true that, for any $F(n)$ such that $F(n)/f(n)\to 0$ for almost all $n$, there are infinitely many $x$ such that\[\frac{\#\{ n\in \mathbb{N} : n+f(n)\in (x,x+F(x))\}}{F(x)}\to \infty?\]

nodes.label: Let $a,b,c\geq 1$ be three integers which are pairwise coprime. Is every large integer the sum of distinct integers of the form $a^kb^lc^m$
nodes.statement: Let $a,b,c\geq 1$ be three integers which are pairwise coprime. Is every large integer the sum of distinct integers of the form $a^kb^lc^m$ ($k,l,m\geq 0$), none of which divide any other?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_123 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ a > 1, ∀ b > 1, ∀ c > 1, PairwiseCoprime a b c →
    IsDComplete (↑(powers a) * ↑(powers b) * ↑(powers c))
nodes.closure_condition: A general theorem proving the set {a^k b^l c^m : k,l,m>=0} is d-complete (every sufficiently large n is a sum of distinct elements, none dividing another) for EVERY pairwise-coprime triple a,b,c>1 — not the existing finite case-checks (Erdos-Lewin 3,5,7; Ma-Chen and Chen-Yu's a=2,b=5/7 and a=3,b=5 ranges). The missing input is a uniform covering/window argument: a constant N and density/antichain construction guaranteeing every integer in an interval (N, K*c*N) is representable as a divisor-free distinct sum of {a^k b^l c^m}, valid for all coprime (a,b,c), which then bootstraps to all large n. Equivalently, a proof that the multiplicative 3-generator semigroup always yields a d-complete antichain-sum cover. Cannot be closed by finite computation.

nodes.label: For any $d\geq 1$ and $k\geq 0$ let $P(d,k)$ be the set of integers which are the sum of distinct powers $d^i$ with $i\geq k$. Let $3\leq d_
nodes.statement: For any $d\geq 1$ and $k\geq 0$ let $P(d,k)$ be the set of integers which are the sum of distinct powers $d^i$ with $i\geq k$. Let $3\leq d_1<d_2<\cdots <d_r$ be integers such that\[\sum_{1\leq i\leq r}\frac{1}{d_r-1}\geq 1.\]Can all sufficiently large integers be written as a sum of the shape $\sum_i c_ia_i$ where $c_i\in \{0,1\}$ and $a_i\in P(d_i,0)$? If we further have $\mathrm{gcd}(d_1,\ldots,d_r)=1$ then, for any $k\geq 1$, can all sufficiently large integers be written as a sum of the shape $\sum_i c_ia_i$ where $c_i\in \{0,1\}$ and $a_i\in P(d_i,k)$?
nodes.formal_statement:
lemma erdos124.zero : answer(True) ↔
    ∀ D : Finset ℕ, (∀ d ∈ D, 3 ≤ d) → 1 ≤ ∑ d ∈ D, (d - 1 : ℚ)⁻¹ →
      ∀ᶠ n in atTop, n ∈ ∑ d ∈ D, sumsOfDistinctPowers d 0
nodes.closure_condition: The remaining open input is the k>=1 ("higher power") case of the Burr-Erdos-Graham-Li conjecture. PRECISE missing lemma: for every integer k>=1 and every finite set of bases 3<=d_1<...<d_r with gcd(d_1,...,d_r)=1 and sum_i 1/(d_i-1) >= 1, prove that all sufficiently large n lie in the sumset sum_i P(d_i,k), where P(d,k) = { sums of distinct powers d^i, i>=k } (i.e. n = sum_i c_i a_i, c_i in {0,1}, a_i divisible by d_i^k with only digits 0,1 in base d_i). Equivalently, formalize/close the `sorry` in `erdos124.ne_zero`. The k=0 / no-gcd first question is SOLVED (Alexeev via Aristotle, Lean `erdos124.zero`); the case {3,4,7} is settled (BEGL96); the Pomerance necessity bound sum 1/(d_i-1)>=1 and gcd=1 necessity are already proven (`erdos124.converse`). So the single bottleneck is the sufficiency direction for general gcd-1 base sets at arbitrary k>=1 — an additive-basis covering statement, NOT finitely checkable (site explicitly: "cannot be resolved with a finite computation").

nodes.label: Let $A = \{ \sum\epsilon_k3^k : \epsilon_k\in \{0,1\}\}$ be the set of integers which have only the digits $0,1$ when written base $3$, and
nodes.statement: Let $A = \{ \sum\epsilon_k3^k : \epsilon_k\in \{0,1\}\}$ be the set of integers which have only the digits $0,1$ when written base $3$, and $B=\{ \sum\epsilon_k4^k : \epsilon_k\in \{0,1\}\}$ be the set of integers which have only the digits $0,1$ when written base $4$. Does $A+B$ have positive lower density?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_125 :
    answer(False) ↔ (A + B).HasPosDensity
nodes.closure_condition: SOLVED (disproved, Lean-verified). Main question — does A+B have positive lower density, where A = base-3 digits {0,1} and B = base-4 digits {0,1}? — answered NO. Closure inputs already supplied: Hasler-Melfi lower bound |C∩[1,x]| ≫ x^0.9777 and lower-density upper bound ≤ 1015/1458 ≈ 0.696; then DeepMind's covering/scaling argument first gave upper density ≥ (6/5)·lower density (killing positive 'density'), then strengthened to lower density = 0 (for any ε>0, infinitely many x with |(A+B)∩[1,x]| < εx). Residual OPEN sub-variants in the Lean file (answer(sorry)): Case 'positive upper density' (0 < upperDensity), Case 1 'zero upper and lower density', and Case 2 'zero lower but positive upper density'. The precise missing input to fully close ALL cases: determine whether the UPPER density of A+B is positive (equivalently pin Case 1 vs Case 2). Finite/explicit closer: an explicit infinite family of windows [1,x_n] with density bound, or a positive lower bound on upperDensity.

nodes.label: Let $f(n)$ be maximal such that if $A\subseteq\mathbb{N}$ has $\lvert A\rvert=n$ then $\prod_{a\neq b\in A}(a+b)$ has at least $f(n)$ distin
nodes.statement: Let $f(n)$ be maximal such that if $A\subseteq\mathbb{N}$ has $\lvert A\rvert=n$ then $\prod_{a\neq b\in A}(a+b)$ has at least $f(n)$ distinct prime factors. Is it true that $f(n)/\log n\to\infty$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_126 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ (f : ℕ → ℕ), IsMaximalAddFactorsCard f →
    Tendsto (fun n => f n / Real.log n) atTop atTop
nodes.closure_condition: A super-logarithmic uniform LOWER bound on f(n): prove that for the maximal f(n) (the guaranteed number of distinct prime factors of the product of pairwise sums prod_{a!=b in A}(a+b) over every n-element A subset of N), one has f(n)/log n -> infinity, strengthening the Erdos-Turan [ErTu34] lower bound f(n) >> log n. Equivalently, a lemma forcing any n-element set's pairwise-sum-set to have omega(prod (a+b)) = omega(log n). (The dual open sub-question erdos_126.variants.isLittleO asks for the matching upper bound f(n)=o(n/log n).) The site states it cannot be resolved by a finite computation.

nodes.label: Let $f(m)$ be maximal such that every graph with $m$ edges must contain a bipartite graph with\[\geq \frac{m}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{8m+1}-1}{8}+f(m
nodes.statement: Let $f(m)$ be maximal such that every graph with $m$ edges must contain a bipartite graph with\[\geq \frac{m}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{8m+1}-1}{8}+f(m)\]edges. Is there an infinite sequence of $m_i$ such that $f(m_i)\to \infty$?

nodes.label: Let $G$ be a graph with $n$ vertices such that every induced subgraph on $\geq \lfloor n/2\rfloor$ vertices has more than $n^2/50$ edges. Mu
nodes.statement: Let $G$ be a graph with $n$ vertices such that every induced subgraph on $\geq \lfloor n/2\rfloor$ vertices has more than $n^2/50$ edges. Must $G$ contain a triangle?

references.label: the entry in the graphs problem collection

nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_128 :
    answer(sorry) ↔ (∀ V' : Set V,
      2 * V'.ncard + 1 ≥ Fintype.card V →
        50 * (G.induce V').edgeSet.ncard > Fintype.card V ^ 2) → ¬ G.CliqueFree 3
nodes.closure_condition: Prove the triangle-forcing density threshold can be lowered from Razborov's proven constant 27/1024 (~0.02637) down to the conjectured-optimal 1/50 = 0.02: i.e. show every n-vertex graph in which every induced subgraph on >= floor(n/2) vertices has > n^2/50 edges must contain a triangle (or, since FALSIFIABLE, exhibit a single finite triangle-free counterexample meeting the n^2/50 induced-density bound). The missing input is a flag-algebra / extremal-density lemma closing the interval (1/50, 27/1024] with the C_5-blowup / Petersen graph as the conjectured tight extremal object.

nodes.label: Let $R(n;k,r)$ be the smallest $N$ such that if the edges of $K_N$ are $r$-coloured then there is a set of $n$ vertices which does not conta
nodes.statement: Let $R(n;k,r)$ be the smallest $N$ such that if the edges of $K_N$ are $r$-coloured then there is a set of $n$ vertices which does not contain a copy of $K_k$ in at least one of the $r$ colours. Prove that there is a constant $C=C(r)>1$ such that\[R(n;3,r) < C^{\sqrt{n}}.\]

nodes.label: Let $A\subset\mathbb{R}^2$ be an infinite set which contains no three points on a line and no four points on a circle. Consider the graph wi
nodes.statement: Let $A\subset\mathbb{R}^2$ be an infinite set which contains no three points on a line and no four points on a circle. Consider the graph with vertices the points in $A$, where two vertices are joined by an edge if and only if they are an integer distance apart. How large can the chromatic number and clique number of this graph be? In particular, can the chromatic number be infinite?

nodes.label: Let $F(N)$ be the maximal size of $A\subseteq\{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that no $a\in A$ divides the sum of any distinct elements of $A\backslash\
nodes.statement: Let $F(N)$ be the maximal size of $A\subseteq\{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that no $a\in A$ divides the sum of any distinct elements of $A\backslash\{a\}$. Estimate $F(N)$. In particular, is it true that\[F(N) > N^{1/2-o(1)}?\]

nodes.label: Let $A\subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be a set of $n$ points. Must there be two distances which occur at least once but between at most $n$ pairs of p
nodes.statement: Let $A\subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be a set of $n$ points. Must there be two distances which occur at least once but between at most $n$ pairs of points? Must the number of such distances $\to \infty$ as $n\to \infty$?

nodes.label: Let $f(n)$ be minimal such that every triangle-free graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and diameter $2$ contains a vertex with degree $\geq f(n)$.
nodes.statement: Let $f(n)$ be minimal such that every triangle-free graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and diameter $2$ contains a vertex with degree $\geq f(n)$. What is the order of growth of $f(n)$? Does $f(n)/\sqrt{n}\to \infty$?

nodes.label: Let $\epsilon,\delta>0$ and $n$ be sufficiently large in terms of $\epsilon$ and $\delta$. Let $G$ be a triangle-free graph on $n$ verti
nodes.statement: Let $\epsilon,\delta>0$ and $n$ be sufficiently large in terms of $\epsilon$ and $\delta$. Let $G$ be a triangle-free graph on $n$ vertices with maximum degree $<n^{1/2-\epsilon}$. Can $G$ be made into a triangle-free graph with diameter $2$ by adding at most $\delta n^2$ edges?

nodes.label: Let $A\subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be a set of $n$ points such that any subset of size $4$ determines at least $5$ distinct distances. Must $A$ det
nodes.statement: Let $A\subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be a set of $n$ points such that any subset of size $4$ determines at least $5$ distinct distances. Must $A$ determine $\gg n^2$ many distances?

nodes.statement: Let $f(n)$ be the smallest number of colours required to colour the edges of $K_n$ such that every $K_4$ contains at least 5 colours. Determine the size of $f(n)$.

nodes.label: We say that $N$ is powerful if whenever $p\mid N$ we also have $p^2\mid N$. Let $k\geq 3$. Can the product of any $k$ consecutive positive i
nodes.statement: We say that $N$ is powerful if whenever $p\mid N$ we also have $p^2\mid N$. Let $k\geq 3$. Can the product of any $k$ consecutive positive integers ever be powerful?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_137 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ k ≥ 3, ∀ n, ¬ (∏ x ∈ Finset.Ioc n (n + k), x).Powerful
nodes.closure_condition: Prove the Erdős–Selfridge conjecture: for every k≥3 and every n, the product N=(n+1)···(n+k) of k consecutive integers is NOT powerful — equivalently, exhibit at least one prime p|N with p²∤N. The precise missing input is a uniform-over-the-window guarantee that the square-full part cannot absorb all k factors at once: an unconditional lower bound forcing some prime to divide exactly one factor (n+i) to the first power only. This is strictly stronger than the proven ES75 'never a perfect power' result (powerful ⊋ perfect-power) and must distinguish k≥3 from k=2 (where n(n+1) IS powerful infinitely often via Pell, problem #364). The cleanest sufficient lemma is Erdős's own sharpening (the multiple_powerful_factors variant): for fixed k and all large n, every product m(m+1)···(m+n) has ≥k distinct primes p with p|N, p²∤N — provable would require an effective count of first-power (square-free-part) primes per consecutive-integer window. No abc-reduction or covering-system argument is known to deliver this.

nodes.label: Let the van der Waerden number $W(k)$ be such that whenever $N\geq W(k)$ and $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ is $2$-coloured there must exist a monochromat
nodes.statement: Let the van der Waerden number $W(k)$ be such that whenever $N\geq W(k)$ and $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ is $2$-coloured there must exist a monochromatic $k$-term arithmetic progression. Improve the bounds for $W(k)$ - for example, prove that $W(k)^{1/k}\to \infty$.
nodes.formal_statement: theorem monoAP_guarantee_set_nonempty (r k) : (monoAP_guarantee_set r k).Nonempty
nodes.closure_condition: A superexponential lower bound on the 2-colour van der Waerden number W(k): specifically prove (or disprove) that W(k)^{1/k} -> infinity, equivalently exhibit W(k) >= c(k)^k with c(k) -> infinity. Current gap is between the lower bound W(k) >> 2^k (Kozik-Shabanov [KoSh16], constant-exponent base 2) and Gowers' tower-type upper bound W(k) <= 2^^{2^^{2^^{2^^{2^{k+9}}}}}. The bottleneck is a base-improving lower-bound construction (a 2-colouring of {1..N} avoiding long monochromatic APs whose AP-free length grows superexponentially in k). The r>=3 analogue W_3(k)^{1/k} >= C^{log* k} was settled by Fox-Hunter [FoHu26]; transferring that hypergraph-container / random-greedy coloring method to r=2 is the natural attack. NOT finite-computation closeable (asymptotic statement; site flags it explicitly).

nodes.label: Let $r_k(N)$ be the size of the largest subset of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ which does not contain a non-trivial $k$-term arithmetic progression. Pro
nodes.statement: Let $r_k(N)$ be the size of the largest subset of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ which does not contain a non-trivial $k$-term arithmetic progression. Prove that $r_k(N)=o(N)$.
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_139 (k : ℕ) (hk : 1 < k) :
    Filter.Tendsto (fun N => (r k N / N : ℝ)) Filter.atTop (𝓝 0)
nodes.closure_condition: SOLVED — Szemerédi's theorem (Szemerédi 1975) proves r_k(N) = o(N) for every fixed k>1, i.e. every k-AP-free subset of {1,...,N} has density tending to 0. Conjectured by Erdős–Turán. Quantitative frontier (NOT part of the closure, only sharpening the o(N) rate): Kelley–Meka 2023 (+Bloom–Sisask) for k=3, Green–Tao 2017 for k=4, Leng–Sah–Sawhney 2024 for k>=5. Lean theorem erdos_139 carries a `sorry` (formalization open) but the mathematical problem is solved.

nodes.label: Let $r_3(N)$ be the size of the largest subset of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ which does not contain a non-trivial $3$-term arithmetic progression. Pro
nodes.statement: Let $r_3(N)$ be the size of the largest subset of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ which does not contain a non-trivial $3$-term arithmetic progression. Prove that $r_3(N)\ll N/(\log N)^C$ for every $C>0$.

nodes.label: Let $k\geq 3$. Are there $k$ consecutive primes in arithmetic progression?
nodes.statement: Let $k\geq 3$. Are there $k$ consecutive primes in arithmetic progression?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem first_three_odd_primes : ({3, 5, 7} : Set ℕ).IsPrimeProgressionOfLength 3

nodes.label: Let $r_k(N)$ be the largest possible size of a subset of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ that does not contain any non-trivial $k$-term arithmetic progress
nodes.statement: Let $r_k(N)$ be the largest possible size of a subset of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ that does not contain any non-trivial $k$-term arithmetic progression. Prove an asymptotic formula for $r_k(N)$.
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_142 (k : ℕ) : (fun N => (r k N : ℝ)) =Θ[atTop] (answer(sorry) : ℕ → ℝ)
nodes.closure_condition: A matching lower bound construction and upper bound for r_k(N) (largest k-AP-free subset of {1,...,N}) that agree to an asymptotic formula — i.e. a function f_k with r_k(N) ~ f_k(N), or even just its order of magnitude. The bottleneck even for k=3: close the gap between the Kelley-Meka upper bound r_3(N) << N exp(-c(log N)^{1/9}) and the Behrend-type lower bound r_3(N) >> N exp(-c'(log N)^{1/2}). No single finite object closes it (site explicitly: cannot be resolved by finite computation); requires a new exponent-matching argument. A weaker named milestone is the $5000 sub-problem r_k(N) = o_k(N/log N).

nodes.label: Let $A\subset (1,\infty)$ be a countably infinite set such that for all $x\neq y\in A$ and integers $k\geq 1$ we have\[ \lvert kx -y\rvert \
nodes.statement: Let $A\subset (1,\infty)$ be a countably infinite set such that for all $x\neq y\in A$ and integers $k\geq 1$ we have\[ \lvert kx -y\rvert \geq 1.\]Does this imply that $A$ is sparse? In particular, does this imply that\[\sum_{x\in A}\frac{1}{x\log x}<\infty\]or\[\sum_{\substack{x <n\\ x\in A}}\frac{1}{x}=o(\log n)?\]
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_143.parts.i : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ (A : Set ℝ), WellSeparatedSet A →
    liminf (fun x => (A ∩ (Set.Icc 1 x)).ncard / x) atTop = 0

nodes.label: The density of integers which have two divisors $d_1,d_2$ such that $d_1<d_2<2d_1$ exists and is equal to $1$.
nodes.statement: The density of integers which have two divisors $d_1,d_2$ such that $d_1<d_2<2d_1$ exists and is equal to $1$.

nodes.label: Let $s_1<s_2<\cdots$ be the sequence of squarefree numbers. Is it true that, for any $\alpha \geq 0$,\[\lim_{x\to \infty}\frac{1}{x}
nodes.statement: Let $s_1<s_2<\cdots$ be the sequence of squarefree numbers. Is it true that, for any $\alpha \geq 0$,\[\lim_{x\to \infty}\frac{1}{x}\sum_{s_n\leq x}(s_{n+1}-s_n)^\alpha\]exists?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_145 :
    answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ α ≥ (0 : ℝ), ∃ β : ℝ,
      atTop.Tendsto (fun x : ℝ ↦ 1 / x * ∑ n ∈ A x, (s (n + 1) - s n : ℝ) ^ α) (𝓝 β)
nodes.closure_condition: An unconditional upper bound on the α-th moment of gaps between consecutive squarefree numbers that pushes existence of lim_{x→∞}(1/x)∑_{s_n≤x}(s_{n+1}-s_n)^α past the current threshold α≤3.75 (Chan) to all α≥0 — equivalently, a strong enough bound on the count/length of squarefree-free intervals (x,x+y). Granville showed the full statement follows from the ABC conjecture, so proving ABC (or an effective squarefree-gap distribution bound of equivalent strength) closes it.

nodes.label: If $H$ is bipartite and is $r$-degenerate, that is, every induced subgraph of $H$ has minimum degree $\leq r$, then\[\mathrm{ex}(n;H) \ll n^
nodes.statement: If $H$ is bipartite and is $r$-degenerate, that is, every induced subgraph of $H$ has minimum degree $\leq r$, then\[\mathrm{ex}(n;H) \ll n^{2-1/r}.\]

nodes.label: If $H$ is bipartite with minimum degree $r$ then there exists $\epsilon=\epsilon(H)>0$ such that\[\mathrm{ex}(n;H) \gg n^{2-\frac{1}{r-1
nodes.statement: If $H$ is bipartite with minimum degree $r$ then there exists $\epsilon=\epsilon(H)>0$ such that\[\mathrm{ex}(n;H) \gg n^{2-\frac{1}{r-1}+\epsilon}.\]

nodes.label: Let $F(k)$ be the number of solutions to\[ 1= \frac{1}{n_1}+\cdots+\frac{1}{n_k},\]where $1\leq n_1<\cdots<n_k$ are distinct integer
nodes.statement: Let $F(k)$ be the number of solutions to\[ 1= \frac{1}{n_1}+\cdots+\frac{1}{n_k},\]where $1\leq n_1<\cdots<n_k$ are distinct integers. Find good estimates for $F(k)$.

nodes.label: The strong chromatic index of a graph $G$, denoted by $\mathrm{sq}(G)$, is the minimum $k$ such that the edges of $G$ can be partitioned int
nodes.statement: The strong chromatic index of a graph $G$, denoted by $\mathrm{sq}(G)$, is the minimum $k$ such that the edges of $G$ can be partitioned into $k$ sets of 'strongly independent' edges, that is, such that the subgraph of $G$ induced by each set is the union of vertex-disjoint edges.Is it true that, for any graph $G$ with maximum degree $\Delta$,\[\mathrm{sq}(G)\leq\frac{5}{4}\Delta^2?\]

nodes.label: A minimal cut of a graph is a minimal set of vertices whose removal disconnects the graph. Let $c(n)$ be the maximum number of minimal cuts
nodes.statement: A minimal cut of a graph is a minimal set of vertices whose removal disconnects the graph. Let $c(n)$ be the maximum number of minimal cuts a graph on $n$ vertices can have. Does $c(n)^{1/n}\to \alpha$ for some $\alpha <2$?

nodes.label: For a graph $G$ let $\tau(G)$ denote the minimal number of vertices that include at least one from each maximal clique of $G$ on at least tw
nodes.statement: For a graph $G$ let $\tau(G)$ denote the minimal number of vertices that include at least one from each maximal clique of $G$ on at least two vertices (sometimes called the clique transversal number).Let $H(n)$ be maximal such that every triangle-free graph on $n$ vertices contains an independent set on $H(n)$ vertices.If $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices then is\[\tau(G)\leq n-H(n)?\]

nodes.label: For any $M\geq 1$, if $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ is a sufficiently large finite Sidon set then there are at least $M$ many $a\in A+A$ such that $
nodes.statement: For any $M\geq 1$, if $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ is a sufficiently large finite Sidon set then there are at least $M$ many $a\in A+A$ such that $a+1,a-1\not\in A+A$.
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_152 : answer(True) ↔ Tendsto f atTop atTop
nodes.closure_condition: SOLVED. For any M≥1, a sufficiently large finite Sidon set A⊂ℕ has at least M "isolated" elements a∈A+A with a−1,a+1∉A+A. Proved in the stronger quantitative form: there are ≫|A|² such isolated representable values (so f(n)→∞ and in fact f(n)≫n²). Both the qualitative (erdos_152) and quadratic variant (erdos_152.variants.square) were formally proved by the DeepMind prover agent [DM26a]/[DM26b] (2026), building on Erdős–Sárközy–Sós [ESS94]. No missing input; the only open residue is the analogous statement for truncations of infinite Sidon sets (flagged as #TODO in the Lean file).

nodes.label: Let $A$ be a finite Sidon set and $A+A=\{s_1<\cdots<s_t\}$. Is it true that\[\frac{1}{t}\sum_{1\leq i<t}(s_{i+1}-s_i)^2 \to \inf
nodes.statement: Let $A$ be a finite Sidon set and $A+A=\{s_1<\cdots<s_t\}$. Is it true that\[\frac{1}{t}\sum_{1\leq i<t}(s_{i+1}-s_i)^2 \to \infty\]as $\lvert A\rvert\to \infty$?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_153 : answer(sorry) ↔ Tendsto f atTop atTop

nodes.label: Let $A\subset \{1,\ldots,N\}$ be a Sidon set with $\lvert A\rvert\sim N^{1/2}$. Must $A+A$ be well-distributed over all small moduli? In par
nodes.statement: Let $A\subset \{1,\ldots,N\}$ be a Sidon set with $\lvert A\rvert\sim N^{1/2}$. Must $A+A$ be well-distributed over all small moduli? In particular, must about half the elements of $A+A$ be even and half odd?

nodes.label: Let $F(N)$ be the size of the largest Sidon subset of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$. Is it true that for every $k\geq 1$ we have\[F(N+k)\leq F(N)+1\]for
nodes.statement: Let $F(N)$ be the size of the largest Sidon subset of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$. Is it true that for every $k\geq 1$ we have\[F(N+k)\leq F(N)+1\]for all sufficiently large $N$?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_155 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ k ≥ 1, ∀ᶠ N in atTop, F (N + k) ≤ F N + 1
nodes.closure_condition: A quantitative increment/stability bound on the extremal Sidon counting function F(N) = max size of a Sidon subset of {1,...,N}: prove that for each fixed k>=1, F(N+k) - F(N) <= 1 for all sufficiently large N. The single most-specific missing input is a window-stability lemma showing the increment over a fixed-width window is eventually at most 1. The sharpest known handle (DesmondWeisenberg forum comment, 29 Aug 2025) reduces this to controlling g(k), where g(N) is the largest size such that two Sidon sets A,B in {1,...,N} exist with (A-A) cap (B-B) = {0} and g(N) <= |A|,|B|; it gives F(N+k) <= F(N) + g(k) for N+1 >= k. So the precise bottleneck is: show that no maximum Sidon set on {1,...,N+k} can carry >= 2 'removable' elements at BOTH the bottom window {1,...,k} and the top window {N+1,...,N+k}, i.e. effectively force the relevant g-quantity down to 1 in the large-N regime. Refuting it requires constructing, for some fixed k and infinitely many N, a Sidon set of {1,...,N+k} that beats every Sidon set of {1,...,N} by >= 2. No finite computation can resolve either direction.

nodes.label: Does there exist a maximal Sidon set $A\subset \{1,\ldots,N\}$ of size $O(N^{1/3})$?
nodes.statement: Does there exist a maximal Sidon set $A\subset \{1,\ldots,N\}$ of size $O(N^{1/3})$?

nodes.label: Does there exist an infinite Sidon set which is an asymptotic basis of order 3?
nodes.statement: Does there exist an infinite Sidon set which is an asymptotic basis of order 3?

nodes.label: Let $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ be an infinite set such that, for any $n$, there are most $2$ solutions to $a+b=n$ with $a\leq b$. Must\[\liminf_{
nodes.statement: Let $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ be an infinite set such that, for any $n$, there are most $2$ solutions to $a+b=n$ with $a\leq b$. Must\[\liminf_{N\to\infty}\frac{\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/2}}=0?\]
nodes.formal_statement:
lemma b2_one {A : Set ℕ} : B2 1 A ↔ IsSidon A where
  mp hA a₁ ha₁ a₂ ha₂ b₁ hb₁ b₂ hb₂ h
nodes.closure_condition: Need either (a) a counting/second-moment lemma proving that every infinite B2[2] set A (at most 2 reps of any n as a+b, a<=b) satisfies |A ∩ [1,N]| = o(N^{1/2}) along some subsequence — i.e. liminf |A∩[1,N]|/N^{1/2}=0, extending the ESS94 Sidon (B2[1]) bound |A∩[1,N]| << N^{1/2}/(log N)^{1/2} to g=2 — OR (b) an explicit infinite B2[2] construction with |A∩[1,N]| >> N^{1/2} for ALL large N (positive lower density at scale N^{1/2}), which would answer NO. The single missing object is a sharp liminf density bound for B2[g], g>=2; the Sidon case is already proved.

nodes.label: There exists some constant $c>0$ such that$$R(C_4,K_n) \ll n^{2-c}.$$
nodes.statement: There exists some constant $c>0$ such that$$R(C_4,K_n) \ll n^{2-c}.$$
nodes.closure_condition: A power-saving upper bound for the off-diagonal Ramsey number R(C_4, K_n): construct/prove some constant c>0 with R(C_4,K_n) << n^{2-c} (equivalently, a near-tight upper bound matching the n^{3/2}/(log n)^{3/2} Spencer lower bound). Concretely, the missing input is an improvement over the current Szemerédi bound R(C_4,K_n) << n^2/(log n)^2 from a polylog saving to a power saving. This reduces to: a quantitative bound showing that every C_4-free graph on N vertices whose independence number is < n must have N << n^{2-c} — i.e. a power-type upper bound on the maximum number of vertices of a C_4-free graph with bounded independence number, sharpening the dependent-random-choice / neighborhood-counting argument (which currently yields only polylog gains) to extract a polynomial gain. No finite computation can settle it; the prize ($100, Er78) is for proof or disproof.

nodes.label: Let $h(N)$ be the smallest $k$ such that $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ can be coloured with $k$ colours so that every four-term arithmetic progression mu
nodes.statement: Let $h(N)$ be the smallest $k$ such that $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ can be coloured with $k$ colours so that every four-term arithmetic progression must contain at least three distinct colours. Estimate $h(N)$.

theorem erdos_160.known_upper :
    (fun n => (erdos_160.h n : ℝ)) =O[atTop] fun n => (n : ℝ) ^ ((2 : ℝ) / 3)

nodes.label: Let $\alpha\in[0,1/2)$ and $n,t\geq 1$. Let $F^{(t)}(n,\alpha)$ be the smallest $m$ such that we can $2$-colour the edges of the complete $t
nodes.statement: Let $\alpha\in[0,1/2)$ and $n,t\geq 1$. Let $F^{(t)}(n,\alpha)$ be the smallest $m$ such that we can $2$-colour the edges of the complete $t$-uniform hypergraph on $n$ vertices such that if $X\subseteq [n]$ with $\lvert X\rvert \geq m$ then there are at least $\alpha \binom{\lvert X\rvert}{t}$ many $t$-subsets of $X$ of each colour. For fixed $n,t$ as we change $\alpha$ from $0$ to $1/2$ does $F^{(t)}(n,\alpha)$ increase continuously or are there jumps? Only one jump?

nodes.statement: Let $\alpha>0$ and $n\geq 1$. Let $F(n,\alpha)$ be the largest $k$ such that there exists some 2-colouring of the edges of $K_n$ in which any induced subgraph $H$ on at least $k$ vertices contains more than $\alpha\binom{\lvert H\rvert}{2}$ many edges of each colour.Prove that for every fixed $0\leq \alpha \leq 1/2$, as $n\to\infty$,\[F(n,\alpha)\sim c_\alpha \log n\]for some constant $c_\alpha$.

nodes.statement: Let $F(N)$ be the size of the largest subset of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ which does not contain any set of the form $\{n,2n,3n\}$. What is\[ \lim_{N\to \infty}\frac{F(N)}{N}?\]Is this limit irrational?

lemma F_0 : F 0 = 0 := rfl
@[category API, AMS 5 11]
lemma F_1 : F 1 = 1 := rfl
@[category API, AMS 5 11]

/--
Sanity check: elements of `IntervalNonTernarySets N` are precisely non ternary subsets of
`{1,...,N}`
-/
@[category API, AMS 5 11]
lemma mem_IntervalNonTernarySets_iff (N : ℕ) (S : Finset ℕ) :

nodes.label: Let $k\geq 3$ and $f(k)$ be the supremum of $\sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n}$ as $A$ ranges over all sets of positive integers which do not contain
nodes.statement: Let $k\geq 3$ and $f(k)$ be the supremum of $\sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n}$ as $A$ ranges over all sets of positive integers which do not contain a $k$-term arithmetic progression. nodes.statement: Let $F(N)$ be the smallest possible size of $A\subset \{0,1,\ldots,N\}$ such that $\{0,1,\ldots,N\}\subset A-A$. Find the value of\[\lim_{N\to \infty}\frac{F(N)}{N^{1/2}}.\]

nodes.closure_condition: Prove the exact value of lim F(N)/sqrt(N) for the (restricted) sparse-ruler / difference-basis problem. Existence of the limit is settled (Erdos-Gal 1948); the gap is closing the interval [1.56..., sqrt(3)]. Concretely: sharpen the lower bound (Leech's 1.56..., from the Redei-Renyi Fourier/density argument) up to sqrt(3), which (with Wichmann's construction giving the sqrt(3) upper bound and Pegg's computation supporting it) would prove lim = sqrt(3). The single missing input is an asymptotic lower-bound lemma F(N) >= (sqrt(3)-o(1))sqrt(N) for restricted difference bases.

nodes.label: Is it true that for every $\epsilon>0$ and integer $t\geq 1$, if $N$ is sufficiently large and $A$ is a subset of $[t]^N$ of size at lea
nodes.statement: Is it true that for every $\epsilon>0$ and integer $t\geq 1$, if $N$ is sufficiently large and $A$ is a subset of $[t]^N$ of size at least $\epsilon t^N$ then $A$ must contain a combinatorial line $P$ (a set $P=\{p_1,\ldots,p_t\}$ where for each coordinate $1\leq j\leq t$ the $j$th coordinate of $p_i$ is either $i$ or constant).

nodes.statement: Is it true that in any finite colouring of $\mathbb{N}$ there exist arbitrarily large finite $A$ such that all sums and products of distinct elements in $A$ are the same colour?

nodes.statement: A finite set $A\subset \mathbb{R}^n$ is called Ramsey if, for any $k\geq 1$, there exists some $d=d(A,k)$ such that in any $k$-colouring of $\mathbb{R}^d$ there exists a monochromatic copy of $A$. Characterise the Ramsey sets in $\mathbb{R}^n$.

nodes.statement: Let $N(k,\ell)$ be the minimal $N$ such that for any $f:\{1,\ldots,N\}\to\{-1,1\}$ there must exist a $k$-term arithmetic progression $P$ such that\[ \left\lvert \sum_{n\in P}f(n)\right\rvert\geq \ell.\]Find good upper bounds for $N(k,\ell)$. Is it true that for any $c>0$ there exists some $C>1$ such that\[N(k,ck)\leq C^k?\]What about\[N(k,2)\leq C^k\]or\[N(k,\sqrt{k})\leq C^k?\]

There exists a function $f:\mathbb{N}\to\{-1,1\}$ such that, for every $d\geq 1$,\[\max_{P_d}\left\lvert \sum_{n\in P_d}f(n)\right\rvert\leq h(d),\]where $P_d$ ranges over all finite arithmetic progressions with common difference $d$.

nodes.statement: Let $A_1,A_2,\ldots$ be an infinite collection of infinite sets of integers, say $A_i=\{a_{i1}<a_{i2}<\cdots\}$. Does there exist some $f:\mathbb{N}\to\{-1,1\}$ such that\[\max_{m, 1\leq i\leq d} \left\lvert \sum_{1\leq j\leq m} f(a_{ij})\right\rvert \ll_d 1\]for all $d\geq 1$?

nodes.label: Let $1\leq k<\ell$ be integers and define $F_k(N,\ell)$ to be minimal such that every set $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ of size $N$ which contains a
nodes.statement: Let $1\leq k<\ell$ be integers and define $F_k(N,\ell)$ to be minimal such that every set $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ of size $N$ which contains at least $F_k(N,\ell)$ many $k$-term arithmetic progressions must contain an $\ell$-term arithmetic progression. Find good upper bounds for $F_k(N,\ell)$. Is it true that\[F_3(N,4)=o(N^2)?\]Is it true that for every $\ell>3$\[\lim_{N\to \infty}\frac{\log F_3(N,\ell)}{\log N}=2?\]

nodes.label: If $\mathcal{F}$ is a finite set of finite graphs then $\mathrm{ex}(n;\mathcal{F})$ is the maximum number of edges a graph on $n$ vertices c
nodes.statement: If $\mathcal{F}$ is a finite set of finite graphs then $\mathrm{ex}(n;\mathcal{F})$ is the maximum number of edges a graph on $n$ vertices can have without containing any subgraphs from $\mathcal{F}$. Note that it is trivial that $\mathrm{ex}(n;\mathcal{F})\leq \mathrm{ex}(n;G)$ for every $G\in\mathcal{F}$. Is it true that, for every $\mathcal{F}$, there exists $G\in\mathcal{F}$ such that\[\mathrm{ex}(n;G)\ll_{\mathcal{F}}\mathrm{ex}(n;\mathcal{F})?\]

nodes.label: Any graph on $n$ vertices can be decomposed into $O(n)$ many edge-disjoint cycles and edges.
nodes.statement: Any graph on $n$ vertices can be decomposed into $O(n)$ many edge-disjoint cycles and edges.
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_184 :
    ∃ f : ℕ → ℝ,
      (f =O[atTop] fun n : ℕ ↦ (n : ℝ)) ∧
      ∀ {V : Type*} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V] (G : SimpleGraph V),
      ∃ (D : Finset G.Subgraph),
        (∀ H ∈ D, IsCycleOrEdge H.coe) ∧
        IsDecomposition G D ∧
        (D.card : ℝ) ≤ f (Fintype.card V)

nodes.label: If $\mathbb{R}^2$ is finitely coloured then must there exist some colour class which contains the vertices of a rectangle of every area?
nodes.statement: If $\mathbb{R}^2$ is finitely coloured then must there exist some colour class which contains the vertices of a rectangle of every area?

theorem erdos_189 :
    answer(False) ↔ Erdos189For
      (fun a b c d ↦
        line[ℝ, a, b].direction ⟂ line[ℝ, b, c].direction ∧
        line[ℝ, b, c].direction ⟂ line[ℝ, c, d].direction ∧
        line[ℝ, c, d].direction ⟂ line[ℝ, d, a].direction)
      (fun a b c d ↦ dist a b * dist b c)

nodes.statement: Let $H(k)$ be the smallest $N$ such that in any finite colouring of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ (into any number of colours) there is always either a monochromatic $k$-term arithmetic progression or a rainbow arithmetic progression (i.e. all elements are different colours). Estimate $H(k)$. Is it true that\[H(k)^{1/k}/k \to \infty\]as $k\to\infty$?

nodes.label: Let $C>0$ be arbitrary. Is it true that, if $n$ is sufficiently large depending on $C$, then in any $2$-colouring of $\binom{\{2,\ldots,
nodes.statement: Let $C>0$ be arbitrary. Is it true that, if $n$ is sufficiently large depending on $C$, then in any $2$-colouring of $\binom{\{2,\ldots,n\}}{2}$ there exists some $X\subseteq \{2,\ldots,n\}$ such that $\binom{X}{2}$ is monochromatic and\[\sum_{x\in X}\frac{1}{\log x}\geq C?\]

nodes.label: Let $A=\{a_1,a_2,\ldots\}\subset \mathbb{R}^d$ be an infinite sequence such that $a_{i+1}-a_i$ is a positive unit vector (i.e. is of the for
nodes.statement: Let $A=\{a_1,a_2,\ldots\}\subset \mathbb{R}^d$ be an infinite sequence such that $a_{i+1}-a_i$ is a positive unit vector (i.e. is of the form $(0,0,\ldots,1,0,\ldots,0)$). For which $d$ must $A$ contain a three-term arithmetic progression?

nodes.label: Let $S\subseteq \mathbb{Z}^3$ be a finite set and let $A=\{a_1,a_2,\ldots,\}\subset \mathbb{Z}^3$ be an infinite $S$-walk, so that $a_{i+1}-
nodes.statement: Let $S\subseteq \mathbb{Z}^3$ be a finite set and let $A=\{a_1,a_2,\ldots,\}\subset \mathbb{Z}^3$ be an infinite $S$-walk, so that $a_{i+1}-a_i\in S$ for all $i$. Must $A$ contain three collinear points?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_193 :
    answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ S : Set (Fin 3 → ℤ), S.Finite →
      /- The statement's $A = \lbrace a_1, a_2, \ldots \rbrace$ is an infinite set.
      If the sequence only takes finitely many values, one value has to repeat infinitely many
      times, which would yield a trivial collinear triple (x, x, x). In this case, the conjecture
      would hold for degenerate S-walks. Another case is constant S-walks, which would render the
      conjecture trivially false (finite loop ranges have no 3 distinct points).
      Assuming the authors intend to stay away from these degenerate cases, we formalize this by
      requiring an infinite range (and require distinct points). -/
      ∀ a : ℕ → Fin 3 → ℤ, IsSWalk S a → (range a).Infinite →
      HasCollinearTriple ℚ (range (fun n ↦ (↑) ∘ a n : ℕ → Fin 3 → ℚ))

nodes.label: Let $k\geq 3$. Must any ordering of $\mathbb{R}$ contain a monotone $k$-term arithmetic progression, that is, some $x_1<\cdots<x_k$
nodes.statement: Let $k\geq 3$. Must any ordering of $\mathbb{R}$ contain a monotone $k$-term arithmetic progression, that is, some $x_1<\cdots<x_k$ which forms an increasing or decreasing $k$-term arithmetic progression?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_194 :
  answer(False) ↔ ∀ k ≥ 3, ∀ r : ℝ → ℝ → Prop, IsStrictTotalOrder ℝ r →
    ∃ s : List ℝ, s.IsAPOfLength k ∧ (s.Pairwise r ∨ s.Pairwise (flip r))

nodes.label: What is the largest $k$ such that in any permutation of $\mathbb{Z}$ there must exist a monotone $k$-term arithmetic progression $x_1<\c
nodes.statement: What is the largest $k$ such that in any permutation of $\mathbb{Z}$ there must exist a monotone $k$-term arithmetic progression $x_1<\cdots<x_k$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_195 :
    answer(sorry) = sSup {k : ℕ | ∀ f : ℤ ≃ ℤ, HasMonotoneAP f k}

nodes.label: Must every permutation of $\mathbb{N}$ contain a monotone 4-term arithmetic progression? In other words, given a permutation $x$ of $\mathbb
nodes.statement: Must every permutation of $\mathbb{N}$ contain a monotone 4-term arithmetic progression? In other words, given a permutation $x$ of $\mathbb{N}$ must there be indices with either $i<j<k<l$ or $i>j>k>l$ such that $x_i,x_j,x_k,x_l$ are an arithmetic progression?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_196 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ (f : ℕ ≃ ℕ), HasMonotoneAP f 4

nodes.statement: Can $\mathbb{N}$ be partitioned into two sets, each of which can be permuted to avoid monotone 3-term arithmetic progressions?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_197 :
    answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ A B : Set ℕ, IsCompl A B ∧
      (∃ f : ℕ ≃ A, ¬HasMonotoneAP (Subtype.val ∘ f) 3) ∧
      (∃ g : ℕ ≃ B, ¬HasMonotoneAP (Subtype.val ∘ g) 3)

nodes.label: If $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ is a Sidon set then must the complement of $A$ contain an infinite arithmetic progression?
nodes.statement: If $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ is a Sidon set then must the complement of $A$ contain an infinite arithmetic progression?

lemma baumgartner_strong (V : Type*) [AddCommGroup V] [Module ℚ V] (k : ℕ) :
    ∃ X : Set V,
      (∀ Y, Y.IsAPOfLength ⊤ → (X ∩ Y).Nonempty) ∧
      (∀ Y, IsAPOfLength Y k → (X ∩ Y).ncard ≤ 2)

nodes.label: If $A\subset \mathbb{R}$ does not contain a 3-term arithmetic progression then must $\mathbb{R}\backslash A$ contain an infinite arithmetic
nodes.statement: If $A\subset \mathbb{R}$ does not contain a 3-term arithmetic progression then must $\mathbb{R}\backslash A$ contain an infinite arithmetic progression?
