nodes.statement: Let $A$ be the set of $n\in \mathbb{N}$ such that there exist $1\leq m_1<\cdots <m_k=n$ with $\sum\tfrac{1}{m_i}=1$. Explore $A$. In particular, does $A$ have density $1$?

nodes.label: Let $k\geq 1$ and let $v(k)$ be the minimal integer which does not appear as some $n_i$ in a solution to\[1=\frac{1}{n_1}+\cdots+\frac{1}{n_
nodes.statement: Let $k\geq 1$ and let $v(k)$ be the minimal integer which does not appear as some $n_i$ in a solution to\[1=\frac{1}{n_1}+\cdots+\frac{1}{n_k}\]with $1\leq n_1<\cdots <n_k$. Estimate the growth of $v(k)$.

nodes.label: Let $N\geq 1$ and let $t(N)$ be the least integer $t$ such that there is no solution to\[1=\frac{1}{n_1}+\cdots+\frac{1}{n_k}\]with $t=n_1&#
nodes.statement: Let $N\geq 1$ and let $t(N)$ be the least integer $t$ such that there is no solution to\[1=\frac{1}{n_1}+\cdots+\frac{1}{n_k}\]with $t=n_1<\cdots <n_k\leq N$. Estimate $t(N)$.

nodes.label: Let $N\geq 1$ and let $k(N)$ denote the smallest $k$ such that there exist $N\leq n_1<\cdots <n_k$ with\[1=\frac{1}{n_1}+\cdots+\fra
nodes.statement: Let $N\geq 1$ and let $k(N)$ denote the smallest $k$ such that there exist $N\leq n_1<\cdots <n_k$ with\[1=\frac{1}{n_1}+\cdots+\frac{1}{n_k}.\]Is it true that\[\lim_{N\to \infty} k(N)-(e-1)N=\infty?\]
nodes.formal_statement:
lemma exists_k (N : ℕ) : ∃ (k : ℕ) (n : Fin k.succ → ℕ),
    (∀ i, N ≤ n i) ∧ StrictMono n ∧ ∑ i, (1 / n i : ℝ) = 1

nodes.label: Let $N\geq 1$ and let $k(N)$ be maximal such that there are $k$ disjoint $A_1,\ldots,A_k\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ with $\sum_{n\in A_i}\frac
nodes.statement: Let $N\geq 1$ and let $k(N)$ be maximal such that there are $k$ disjoint $A_1,\ldots,A_k\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ with $\sum_{n\in A_i}\frac{1}{n}=1$ for all $i$. Estimate $k(N)$. Is it true that $k(N)=o(\log N)$?

nodes.label: Let $N\geq 1$. How many $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ are there such that $\sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n}=1$?
nodes.statement: Let $N\geq 1$. How many $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ are there such that $\sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n}=1$?

nodes.label: Does every set $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ of positive density contain some finite $S\subset A$ such that $\sum_{n\in S}\frac{1}{n}=1$?
nodes.statement: Does every set $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ of positive density contain some finite $S\subset A$ such that $\sum_{n\in S}\frac{1}{n}=1$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_299 : answer(False) ↔ (∃ (a : ℕ → ℕ),
    StrictMono a ∧ (∀ n, 0 < a n) ∧
    (fun n ↦ (a (n + 1) : ℝ) - a n) =O[atTop] (1 : ℕ → ℝ) ∧
    ∀ S : Finset ℕ, ∑ i ∈ S, (1 : ℝ) / a i ≠ 1)

nodes.label: Is there an infinite sequence $a_1<a_2<\cdots $ such that $a_{i+1}-a_i=O(1)$ and no finite sum of $\frac{1}{a_i}$ is equal to $1$?
nodes.statement: Is there an infinite sequence $a_1<a_2<\cdots $ such that $a_{i+1}-a_i=O(1)$ and no finite sum of $\frac{1}{a_i}$ is equal to $1$?

nodes.label: Let $A(N)$ denote the maximal cardinality of $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that $\sum_{n\in S}\frac{1}{n}\neq 1$ for all $S\subseteq A$.
nodes.statement: Let $A(N)$ denote the maximal cardinality of $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that $\sum_{n\in S}\frac{1}{n}\neq 1$ for all $S\subseteq A$. Estimate $A(N)$.

nodes.label: Let $f(N)$ be the size of the largest $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that there are no solutions to\[\frac{1}{a}= \frac{1}{b_1}+\cdots+\fr
nodes.statement: Let $f(N)$ be the size of the largest $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that there are no solutions to\[\frac{1}{a}= \frac{1}{b_1}+\cdots+\frac{1}{b_k}\]with distinct $a,b_1,\ldots,b_k\in A$?Estimate $f(N)$. In particular, is it true that $f(N)=(\tfrac{1}{2}+o(1))N$?
nodes.closure_condition: A matching upper bound f(N) ≤ (1/2 + o(1))N for the largest A ⊆ {1,...,N} with no distinct solution to 1/a = 1/b_1 + ... + 1/b_k. Concretely: a covering of {1,...,N} (asymptotically all but o(N) elements) by a disjoint family of finite "obstruction blocks" S — each block being a set of integers that contains a distinct unit-fraction relation 1/a = sum 1/b_i — such that the per-block forced-deletion count summed over the family forces |A| ≤ (1/2+o(1))N. The current best block family S_a = {2a,3a,4a,6a,12a} (a of form 8^b 9^c d, (d,6)=1) only forces the 25/28 bound; the missing input is a richer disjoint family of multiplicative relation-blocks whose density of forced deletions reaches N/2. (If instead the conjecture is FALSE, the closure is an explicit construction of A of size (1/2+c)N, c>0, dilation-free of distinct Egyptian-fraction relations.)

nodes.label: Let $f(N)$ be the size of the largest $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that there are no solutions to\[\frac{1}{a}= \frac{1}{b}+\frac{1}{c}\
nodes.statement: Let $f(N)$ be the size of the largest $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that there are no solutions to\[\frac{1}{a}= \frac{1}{b}+\frac{1}{c}\]with distinct $a,b,c\in A$?Estimate $f(N)$. In particular, is $f(N)=(\tfrac{1}{2}+o(1))N$?
nodes.closure_condition: A density (supersaturation) upper bound proving f(N) ≤ (1/2+o(1))N for the largest A⊆{1,…,N} avoiding distinct solutions of 1/a=1/b+1/c — i.e., that any A with |A| ≥ (1/2+ε)N must contain a distinct triple a,b,c with c·b = a(b+c) (equivalently 1/a=1/b+1/c). The matching lower bound (1/2+o(1))N is already attained (all odds, or [N/2,N]); current bounds straddle the truth at LB=(5/8+o(1))N (Cambie) and UB=(9/10+o(1))N (van Doorn). The missing input is the exact extremal constant 1/2: a counting/density-increment argument that the only near-extremal configurations are the trivial ones, closing the [5/8, 9/10] gap. A construction beating 1/2 would instead refute the conjecture. NOTE: this is an existence-of-structure density problem, not a finite/abc-style computation — explicitly flagged as 'cannot be resolved with a finite computation.'

nodes.label: Is it true that in any finite colouring of the integers there exists a monochromatic solution to\[\frac{1}{a}=\frac{1}{b}+\frac{1}{c}\]with
nodes.statement: Is it true that in any finite colouring of the integers there exists a monochromatic solution to\[\frac{1}{a}=\frac{1}{b}+\frac{1}{c}\]with distinct $a,b,c$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_303 :
    answer(True) ↔
    -- For any finite colouring of the integers
    ∀ (𝓒 : ℤ → ℤ), (Set.range 𝓒).Finite →
      -- There exists integers `a, b, c`
      ∃ (a b c : ℤ),
      -- that are non-zero and distinct.
      [a, b, c, 0].Nodup ∧
      -- `a, b, c` satisfy the equation
      (1/a : ℝ) = 1/b + 1/c ∧
      -- `a, b, c` have the same color
      (𝓒 '' {a, b, c}).Subsingleton

nodes.label: For integers $1\leq a<b$ let $N(a,b)$ denote the minimal $k$ such that there exist integers $1<n_1<\cdots<n_k$ with\[\frac{a
nodes.statement: For integers $1\leq a<b$ let $N(a,b)$ denote the minimal $k$ such that there exist integers $1<n_1<\cdots<n_k$ with\[\frac{a}{b}=\frac{1}{n_1}+\cdots+\frac{1}{n_k}.\]Estimate $N(b)=\max_{1\leq a<b}N(a,b)$. Is it true that $N(b) \ll \log\log b$?
nodes.formal_statement:
lemma zero_mem_unitFractionExpressible_iff {a b : ℕ} :
    0 ∈ unitFractionExpressible a b ↔ a = 0 ∨ b = 0
nodes.closure_condition: Tighten the gap on N(b) = max_{1≤a<b} N(a,b), the minimal number of distinct unit fractions needed to write a/b. Specifically, prove (or refute) the conjectured upper bound N(b) ≪ log log b. The bottleneck is improving Vose's 1985 bound N(b) ≪ √(log b) all the way down to the Erdős lower bound log log b ≪ N(b): the missing input is a greedy/recursive Egyptian-fraction decomposition algorithm whose worst-case denominator count over all a<b is O(log log b), matched against the existing log log b lower bound. No finite computation can settle it (asymptotic in b).

nodes.label: For integers $1\leq a<b$ let $D(a,b)$ be the minimal value of $n_k$ such that there exist integers $1\leq n_1<\cdots <n_k$ with\
nodes.statement: For integers $1\leq a<b$ let $D(a,b)$ be the minimal value of $n_k$ such that there exist integers $1\leq n_1<\cdots <n_k$ with\[\frac{a}{b}=\frac{1}{n_1}+\cdots+\frac{1}{n_k}.\]Estimate $D(b)=\max_{1\leq a<b}D(a,b)$. Is it true that\[D(b) \ll b(\log b)^{1+o(1)}?\]

nodes.label: Let $a/b\in \mathbb{Q}_{>0}$ with $b$ squarefree. Are there integers $1<n_1<\cdots<n_k$, each the product of two distinct pr
nodes.statement: Let $a/b\in \mathbb{Q}_{>0}$ with $b$ squarefree. Are there integers $1<n_1<\cdots<n_k$, each the product of two distinct primes, such that\[\frac{a}{b}=\frac{1}{n_1}+\cdots+\frac{1}{n_k}?\]
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_306 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ (q : ℚ), 0 < q → Squarefree q.den →
    ∃ k : ℕ, ∃ (n : Fin (k + 1) → ℕ), n 0 = 1 ∧ StrictMono n ∧
    (∀ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 (Fin.last k), ω (n i) = 2 ∧ Ω (n i) = 2) ∧
    q = ∑ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 (Fin.last k), (1 : ℚ) / (n i)
nodes.closure_condition: A general existence theorem: prove (or refute) that every a/b in Q_{>0} with b squarefree admits an Egyptian-fraction representation 1/n_1+...+1/n_k with strictly increasing n_i that are each a product of exactly two distinct primes (semiprimes). The precise missing input is an extension of the Butler-Erdos-Graham [BEG15] splitting/replacement construction — which handles the THREE-distinct-primes case for integer targets (b=1) — down to TWO prime factors and to arbitrary squarefree b. Concretely: a constructive lemma showing the reciprocals of semiprimes {1/(pq)} are "flexible enough" (sufficient density plus a denominator-replacement / continued-splitting step) to realize any squarefree-denominator rational, OR a structural obstruction (a congruence/p-adic or denominator-parity invariant on sums of distinct semiprime reciprocals) proving some such a/b is unreachable. Existing 1=sum constructions (Barbeau, Burshtein, Johnson/A201650, Watanabe 47-term) are single-target witnesses and do not close the general case.

nodes.label: Are there two finite sets of primes $P,Q$ such that\[1=\left(\sum_{p\in P}\frac{1}{p}\right)\left(\sum_{q\in Q}\frac{1}{q}\right)?\]
nodes.statement: Are there two finite sets of primes $P,Q$ such that\[1=\left(\sum_{p\in P}\frac{1}{p}\right)\left(\sum_{q\in Q}\frac{1}{q}\right)?\]
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_307 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ P Q : Finset ℕ, (∀ p ∈ P, p.Prime) ∧ (∀ q ∈ Q, q.Prime) ∧
    1 = (∑ p ∈ P, (p : ℚ)⁻¹) * (∑ q ∈ Q, (q : ℚ)⁻¹)
nodes.closure_condition: Either (a) an explicit finite witness: two disjoint sets of primes P, Q with (Σ_{p∈P} 1/p)(Σ_{q∈Q} 1/q) = 1 — a finite construction that closes the problem affirmatively (the site explicitly tags it VERIFIABLE = provable by a finite example, with |P∪Q| ≥ 60 forced); OR (b) a structural impossibility proof showing no such prime pair exists. The precise bottleneck is the p-adic balancing obstruction: when the two prime-reciprocal sums A = a/D_P and B = b/D_Q are multiplied, AB = 1 requires a·b = D_P·D_Q, i.e. the numerators must absorb every prime power in the common denominators. Since each prime p∈P divides D_P to first order but the numerator a = Σ (D_P/p) has p∤a unless special cancellation occurs, one needs a simultaneous system of valuation/divisibility conditions ν_p(ab) = ν_p(D_P D_Q) for all primes p in play. The missing input is a proof that this valuation system is satisfiable (yielding a construction) or unsatisfiable (yielding impossibility) for finite prime sets. No theorem currently rules either way; the affirmative side is a bounded but astronomically large search (≥60 primes), the negative side needs a new arithmetic obstruction.

nodes.label: Let $N\geq 1$. What is the smallest integer not representable as the sum of distinct unit fractions with denominators from $\{1,\ldots,N\}$?
nodes.statement: Let $N\geq 1$. What is the smallest integer not representable as the sum of distinct unit fractions with denominators from $\{1,\ldots,N\}$? Is it true that the set of integers representable as such has the shape $\{1,\ldots,m\}$ for some $m$?

nodes.label: Let $N\geq 1$. How many integers can be written as the sum of distinct unit fractions with denominators from $\{1,\ldots,N\}$? Are there $o(
nodes.statement: Let $N\geq 1$. How many integers can be written as the sum of distinct unit fractions with denominators from $\{1,\ldots,N\}$? Are there $o(\log N)$ such integers?

nodes.label: Let $\alpha >0$ and $N\geq 1$. Is it true that for any $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ with $\lvert A\rvert \geq \alpha N$ there exists some
nodes.statement: Let $\alpha >0$ and $N\geq 1$. Is it true that for any $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ with $\lvert A\rvert \geq \alpha N$ there exists some $S\subseteq A$ such that\[\frac{a}{b}=\sum_{n\in S}\frac{1}{n}\]with $a\leq b =O_\alpha(1)$?

nodes.label: Let $\delta(N)$ be the minimal non-zero value of $\lvert 1-\sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n}\rvert$ as $A$ ranges over all subsets of $\{1,\ldots,N\}
nodes.statement: Let $\delta(N)$ be the minimal non-zero value of $\lvert 1-\sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n}\rvert$ as $A$ ranges over all subsets of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$. Is it true that\[\delta(N)=e^{-(c+o(1))N}\]for some constant $c\in (0,1)$?

nodes.label: Does there exist some $c>0$ such that, for any $K>1$, whenever $A$ is a sufficiently large finite multiset of positive integers with
nodes.statement: Does there exist some $c>0$ such that, for any $K>1$, whenever $A$ is a sufficiently large finite multiset of positive integers with $\sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n}>K$ there exists some $S\subseteq A$ such that\[1-e^{-cK} < \sum_{n\in S}\frac{1}{n}\leq 1?\]
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_312 :
    answer(sorry) ↔
    ∃ (c : ℝ), 0 < c ∧
      ∀ (K : ℝ), 1 < K →
        ∃ (N₀ : ℕ),
          ∀ (n : ℕ) (a : Fin n → ℕ),
            (n ≥ N₀ ∧ (∑ i : Fin n, (a i : ℝ)⁻¹) > K) →
              ∃ (S : Finset (Fin n)),
                1 - Real.exp (-(c * K)) < (∑ i ∈ S, (a i : ℝ)⁻¹) ∧
                ∑ i ∈ S, (a i : ℝ)⁻¹ ≤ 1
nodes.closure_condition: An exponential-rate "dense subset-sum near 1" theorem: a constant c>0 such that every sufficiently large finite multiset A of positive integers with R(A)=Σ_{n∈A}1/n > K admits a sub-multiset S with reciprocal sum in (1−e^{−cK}, 1]. Equivalently, prove the best under-approximation satisfies ε(A) := min{1−R(S) : S⊆A, R(S)≤1} ≤ e^{−c·R(A)}. The precise missing input is the IMPROVEMENT of the Erdős–Graham polynomial bound ε(A) ≤ c/K² (known) to an EXPONENTIAL bound ε(A) ≤ e^{−cK}. The bottleneck is a structural lemma showing the achievable sub-sums of A form an exponentially fine net just below 1 — e.g. via a probabilistic/Fourier-analytic concentration argument controlling the granularity of partial reciprocal sums, robust to multiset multiplicities (the primorial construction A=⋃_{p≤N}{p^{(p−1)}} caps the rate at e^{−(1+o(1))K log K}, so the target exponent is essentially optimal in order).

nodes.label: Are there infinitely many solutions to\[\frac{1}{p_1}+\cdots+\frac{1}{p_k}=1-\frac{1}{m},\]where $m\geq 2$ is an integer and $p_1<\cdots
nodes.statement: Are there infinitely many solutions to\[\frac{1}{p_1}+\cdots+\frac{1}{p_k}=1-\frac{1}{m},\]where $m\geq 2$ is an integer and $p_1<\cdots<p_k$ are distinct primes?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_313 : answer(sorry) ↔ erdos313Solutions.Infinite

nodes.label: Let $n\geq 1$ and let $m$ be minimal such that $\sum_{n\leq k\leq m}\frac{1}{k}\geq 1$. We define\[\epsilon(n) = \sum_{n\leq k\leq m}\frac{1
nodes.statement: Let $n\geq 1$ and let $m$ be minimal such that $\sum_{n\leq k\leq m}\frac{1}{k}\geq 1$. We define\[\epsilon(n) = \sum_{n\leq k\leq m}\frac{1}{k}-1.\]How small can $\epsilon(n)$ be? Is it true that\[\liminf n^2\epsilon(n)=0?\]

nodes.label: Let $u_1=1$ and $u_{n+1}=u_n(u_n+1)$, so that $\sum_{k\geq 1}\frac{1}{u_k+1}$ and $u_k=\lfloor c_0^{2^k}+1\rfloor$ for $k\geq 1$, where\[c_0
nodes.statement: Let $u_1=1$ and $u_{n+1}=u_n(u_n+1)$, so that $\sum_{k\geq 1}\frac{1}{u_k+1}$ and $u_k=\lfloor c_0^{2^k}+1\rfloor$ for $k\geq 1$, where\[c_0=\lim u_n^{1/2^n}=1.264085\cdots.\]Let $a_1<a_2<\cdots $ be any other sequence with $\sum \frac{1}{a_k}=1$. Is it true that\[\liminf a_n^{1/2^n}<c_0=1.264085\cdots?\]

nodes.label: Is it true that if $A\subset \mathbb{N}\backslash\{1\}$ is a finite set with $\sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n}<2$ then there is a partition $A=A
nodes.statement: Is it true that if $A\subset \mathbb{N}\backslash\{1\}$ is a finite set with $\sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n}<2$ then there is a partition $A=A_1\sqcup A_2$ such that\[\sum_{n\in A_i}\frac{1}{n}<1\]for $i=1,2$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_316 : answer(False) ↔ ∀ A : Finset ℕ, 0 ∉ A → 1 ∉ A →
    ∑ n ∈ A, (1 / n : ℚ) < 2 → ∃ (A₁ A₂ : Finset ℕ),
      Disjoint A₁ A₂ ∧ A = A₁ ∪ A₂ ∧
      ∑ n ∈ A₁, (1 / n : ℚ) < 1 ∧ ∑ n ∈ A₂, (1 / n : ℚ) <  1

nodes.label: Is there some constant $c>0$ such that for every $n\geq 1$ there exists some $\delta_k\in \{-1,0,1\}$ for $1\leq k\leq n$ with\[0< \left
nodes.statement: Is there some constant $c>0$ such that for every $n\geq 1$ there exists some $\delta_k\in \{-1,0,1\}$ for $1\leq k\leq n$ with\[0< \left\lvert \sum_{1\leq k\leq n}\frac{\delta_k}{k}\right\rvert < \frac{c}{2^n}?\]Is it true that for sufficiently large $n$, for any $\delta_k\in \{-1,0,1\}$,\[\left\lvert \sum_{1\leq k\leq n}\frac{\delta_k}{k}\right\rvert > \frac{1}{[1,\ldots,n]}\]whenever the left-hand side is not zero?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_317 : answer(sorry) ↔
    ∃ c > 0, ∀ n ≥ 1, ∃ δ : Fin n → ℚ,
      Set.range δ ⊆ {-1, 0, 1} ∧
      letI lhs : ℝ := |∑ k, (δ k) / (k + 1)|
      0 < lhs ∧ lhs < c / 2^n

nodes.label: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be an infinite arithmetic progression and $f:A\to \{-1,1\}$ be a non-constant function. Must there exist a finit
nodes.statement: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be an infinite arithmetic progression and $f:A\to \{-1,1\}$ be a non-constant function. Must there exist a finite non-empty $S\subset A$ such that\[\sum_{n\in S}\frac{f(n)}{n}=0?\]What about if $A$ is an arbitrary set of positive density? What if $A$ is the set of squares excluding $1$?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_318.variants.univ : P₁ univ

nodes.label: What is the size of the largest $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that there is a function $\delta:A\to \{-1,1\}$ such that\[\sum_{n\in A}\fr
nodes.statement: What is the size of the largest $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that there is a function $\delta:A\to \{-1,1\}$ such that\[\sum_{n\in A}\frac{\delta_n}{n}=0\]and\[\sum_{n\in A'}\frac{\delta_n}{n}\neq 0\]for all non-empty $A'\subsetneq A$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_319 (N : ℕ) : IsGreatest
    { #A | (A) (_ : A ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N)
      (_ : ∃ δ : ℕ → ℤˣ, ∑ n ∈ A, (δ n : ℚ) / n = 0 ∧
        ∀ A' ⊂ A, A'.Nonempty → ∑ n ∈ A', (δ n : ℚ) / n ≠ 0) }
    answer(sorry)
nodes.closure_condition: The missing input is the true asymptotic order/constant of c(N) = max size of A⊆{1,…,N} admitting signs δ:A→{±1} with Σδ_n/n = 0 but no nonempty proper subset having vanishing signed sum (a "minimal/irreducible" signed zero-sum). Only a lower bound c(N) ≥ (1−1/e+o(1))N is known (Adenwalla, via Croot's [Cr01] theorem that some B⊂[(1/e−o(1))N, N] has Σ1/b = 1). CLOSURE requires EITHER (a) a matching construction pushing the lower bound to (1+o(1))N — i.e. showing almost all of {1,…,N} can be assembled into a single minimal signed zero-sum, likely via an improved dense short-interval unit-fraction representation covering [εN,N] for arbitrarily small ε while preserving minimality — OR (b) a nontrivial upper bound c(N) ≤ (1−c)N for some constant c>0, i.e. a proof that the minimality constraint (no vanishing proper signed subsum) forces a positive density of {1,…,N} to be excluded. The specific bottleneck lemma is a density-cost bound on the irreducibility constraint for signed Egyptian-fraction sums, which no current technique supplies.

nodes.label: Let $S(N)$ count the number of distinct sums of the form $\sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n}$ for $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$. Estimate $S(N)$.
nodes.statement: Let $S(N)$ count the number of distinct sums of the form $\sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n}$ for $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$. Estimate $S(N)$.

nodes.label: What is the size of the largest $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that all sums $\sum_{n\in S}\frac{1}{n}$ are distinct for $S\subseteq A$?
nodes.statement: What is the size of the largest $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that all sums $\sum_{n\in S}\frac{1}{n}$ are distinct for $S\subseteq A$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_321 (N : ℕ) :
    R N = answer(sorry)

nodes.label: Let $k\geq 3$ and $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ be the set of $k$th powers. What is the order of growth of $1_A^{(k)}(n)$, i.e. the number of repres
nodes.statement: Let $k\geq 3$ and $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ be the set of $k$th powers. What is the order of growth of $1_A^{(k)}(n)$, i.e. the number of representations of $n$ as the sum of $k$ many $k$th powers? Does there exist some $c>0$ and infinitely many $n$ such that\[1_A^{(k)}(n) >n^c?\]

nodes.label: Let $1\leq m\leq k$ and $f_{k,m}(x)$ denote the number of integers $\leq x$ which are the sum of $m$ many nonnegative $k$th powers. Is it tr
nodes.statement: Let $1\leq m\leq k$ and $f_{k,m}(x)$ denote the number of integers $\leq x$ which are the sum of $m$ many nonnegative $k$th powers. Is it true that\[f_{k,k}(x) \gg_\epsilon x^{1-\epsilon}\]for all $\epsilon>0$? Is it true that if $m<k$ then\[f_{k,m}(x) \gg x^{m/k}\]for sufficiently large $x$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_323.parts.i :
    answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ k ≥ 1, ∀ ε > (0 : ℝ),
      (fun (x : ℕ) ↦ (x : ℝ) ^ (1 - ε)) =O[atTop] (fun (x : ℕ) ↦ (f k k x : ℝ))

nodes.label: Does there exist a polynomial $f(x)\in\mathbb{Z}[x]$ such that all the sums $f(a)+f(b)$ with $a<b$ nonnegative integers are distinct?
nodes.statement: Does there exist a polynomial $f(x)\in\mathbb{Z}[x]$ such that all the sums $f(a)+f(b)$ with $a<b$ nonnegative integers are distinct?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_324 : answer(sorry) ↔
    ∃ f : ℤ[X], {(a, b) : ℕ × ℕ | a < b}.InjOn fun (a, b) => f.eval (a : ℤ) + f.eval (b : ℤ)

nodes.label: Let $k\geq 3$ and $f_{k,3}(x)$ denote the number of integers $\leq x$ which are the sum of three nonnegative $k$th powers. Is it true that\[
nodes.statement: Let $k\geq 3$ and $f_{k,3}(x)$ denote the number of integers $\leq x$ which are the sum of three nonnegative $k$th powers. Is it true that\[f_{k,3}(x) \gg x^{3/k}\]or even $\gg_\epsilon x^{3/k-\epsilon}$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_325 :
     answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ k : ℕ, 3 ≤ k → (fun x : ℕ => (x : ℝ) ^ (3 / k : ℝ)) =O[atTop]
      (fun x : ℕ => (cardIsSumThreePowerBelow k x : ℝ))

nodes.label: Does there exist $A=\{a_1<a_2<\cdots\}\subset \mathbb{N}$ which is a minimal basis of order $2$ (i.e. every large integer is the sum
nodes.statement: Does there exist $A=\{a_1<a_2<\cdots\}\subset \mathbb{N}$ which is a minimal basis of order $2$ (i.e. every large integer is the sum of $2$ elements from $A$, and no proper subset of $A$ has this property), such that\[\lim_{k\to \infty}\frac{a_k}{k^2}=c\]for some $c\neq 0$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_326 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ (A : Set ℕ), A.IsAddBasisOfOrder 2 →
    ∃ (b : ℕ → ℕ), StrictMono b ∧ ∀ n, b n ∈ A ∧ (Set.range b).IsAddBasis ∧
      ∀ (x : ℝ), ¬ Tendsto (fun n ↦ (b n : ℝ) / n ^ 2) atTop (𝓝 x)

nodes.label: Suppose $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ is such that if $a,b\in A$ and $a\neq b$ then $a+b\nmid ab$. Can $A$ be 'substantially more' than the od
nodes.statement:
Suppose $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ is such that if $a,b\in A$ and $a
eq b$ then $a+b
mid ab$. Can $A$ be 'substantially more' than the odd numbers?What if $a,b\in A$ with $a
eq b$ implies $a+b
mid 2ab$? Must $\lvert Avert=o(N)$?

nodes.label: Suppose $A\subseteq\mathbb{N}$ and $C>0$ is such that $1_A\ast 1_A(n)\leq C$ for all $n\in\mathbb{N}$. Can $A$ be partitioned into $t$ m
nodes.statement: Suppose $A\subseteq\mathbb{N}$ and $C>0$ is such that $1_A\ast 1_A(n)\leq C$ for all $n\in\mathbb{N}$. Can $A$ be partitioned into $t$ many subsets $A_1,\ldots,A_t$ (where $t=t(C)$ depends only on $C$) such that $1_{A_i}\ast 1_{A_i}(n)<C$ for all $1\leq i\leq t$ and $n\in \mathbb{N}$?

nodes.label: Suppose $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is a Sidon set. How large can\[\limsup_{N\to \infty}\frac{\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/2}}\]be?
nodes.statement: Suppose $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is a Sidon set. How large can\[\limsup_{N\to \infty}\frac{\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/2}}\]be?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_329 : sSup {sidonUpperDensity A | (A : Set ℕ) (_ : IsSidon A)} =
    answer(sorry)

nodes.label: Does there exist a minimal basis with positive density, say $A\subset\mathbb{N}$, such that for any $n\in A$ the (upper) density of integers
nodes.statement: Does there exist a minimal basis with positive density, say $A\subset\mathbb{N}$, such that for any $n\in A$ the (upper) density of integers which cannot be represented without using $n$ is positive?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_330_statement :
    answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ (A : Set ℕ), ∃ h, MinAsymptoticAddBasisOfOrder A h ∧ A.HasPosDensity ∧
    ∀ n ∈ A, Set.HasPosDensity (UnrepWithout A n h)

nodes.label: Let $A,B\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ such that for all large $N$\[\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert \gg N^{1/2}\]and\[\lvert B\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\r
nodes.statement: Let $A,B\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ such that for all large $N$\[\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert \gg N^{1/2}\]and\[\lvert B\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert \gg N^{1/2}.\]Is it true that there are infinitely many solutions to $a_1-a_2=b_1-b_2\neq 0$ with $a_1,a_2\in A$ and $b_1,b_2\in B$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_331 :
    answer(False) ↔
      ∀ A B : Set ℕ,
      (fun (n : ℕ) ↦ (n : ℝ) ^ (1 / 2 : ℝ)) =O[atTop] (fun (n : ℕ) ↦ (count A n : ℝ)) →
      (fun (n : ℕ) ↦ (n : ℝ) ^ (1 / 2 : ℝ)) =O[atTop] (fun (n : ℕ) ↦ (count B n : ℝ)) →
      { s : ℕ × ℕ × ℕ × ℕ | let ⟨a₁, a₂, b₁, b₂⟩ := s
        a₁ ∈ A ∧ a₂ ∈ A ∧ b₁ ∈ B ∧ b₂ ∈ B ∧
        a₁ ≠ a₂ ∧ a₁ + b₂ = a₂ + b₁ }.Infinite

nodes.label: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ and $D(A)$ be the set of those numbers which occur infinitely often as $a_1-a_2$ with $a_1,a_2\in A$. What condi
nodes.statement: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ and $D(A)$ be the set of those numbers which occur infinitely often as $a_1-a_2$ with $a_1,a_2\in A$. What conditions on $A$ are sufficient to ensure $D(A)$ has bounded gaps?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_332 (A : Set ℕ) : (answer(sorry) : Set ℕ → Prop) A → HasBoundedGaps (D_A A)

nodes.label: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be a set of density zero. Does there exist a $B$ such that $A\subseteq B+B$ and\[\lvert B\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rve
nodes.statement: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be a set of density zero. Does there exist a $B$ such that $A\subseteq B+B$ and\[\lvert B\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert =o(N^{1/2})\]for all large $N$?

nodes.label: Find the best function $f(n)$ such that every $n$ can be written as $n=a+b$ where both $a,b$ are $f(n)$-smooth (that is, are not divisible b
nodes.statement: Find the best function $f(n)$ such that every $n$ can be written as $n=a+b$ where both $a,b$ are $f(n)$-smooth (that is, are not divisible by any prime $p>f(n)$.)

nodes.label: Let $d(A)$ denote the density of $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$. Characterise those $A,B\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ with positive density such that\[d(A+
nodes.statement: Let $d(A)$ denote the density of $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$. Characterise those $A,B\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ with positive density such that\[d(A+B)=d(A)+d(B).\]

nodes.label: For $r\geq 2$ let $h(r)$ be the maximal finite $k$ such that there exists a basis $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ of order $r$ (so every large integ
nodes.statement: For $r\geq 2$ let $h(r)$ be the maximal finite $k$ such that there exists a basis $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ of order $r$ (so every large integer is the sum of at most $r$ integers from $A$) and exact order $k$ (so every large integer is the sum of exactly $k$ integers from $A$). Find the value of\[\lim_r \frac{h(r)}{r^2}.\]

nodes.label: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be an additive basis (of any finite order) such that $\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert=o(N)$. Is it true that\[
nodes.statement: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be an additive basis (of any finite order) such that $\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert=o(N)$. Is it true that\[\lim_{N\to \infty}\frac{\lvert (A+A)\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}=\infty?\]

nodes.label: The restricted order of a basis is the least integer $t$ (if it exists) such that every large integer is the sum of at most $t$ distinct sum
nodes.statement: The restricted order of a basis is the least integer $t$ (if it exists) such that every large integer is the sum of at most $t$ distinct summands from $A$. What are necessary and sufficient conditions that this exists? Can it be bounded (when it exists) in terms of the order of the basis? What are necessary and sufficient conditions that this is equal to the order of the basis?

nodes.label: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be a basis of order $r$. Must the set of integers representable as the sum of exactly $r$ distinct elements from
nodes.statement: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be a basis of order $r$. Must the set of integers representable as the sum of exactly $r$ distinct elements from $A$ have positive lower density?

nodes.label: Let $A=\{1,2,4,8,13,21,31,45,66,81,97,\ldots\}$ be the greedy Sidon sequence: we begin with $1$ and iteratively include the next smallest in
nodes.statement: Let $A=\{1,2,4,8,13,21,31,45,66,81,97,\ldots\}$ be the greedy Sidon sequence: we begin with $1$ and iteratively include the next smallest integer that preserves the Sidon property (i.e. there are no non-trivial solutions to $a+b=c+d$). What is the order of growth of $A$? Is it true that\[\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert \gg N^{1/2-\epsilon}\]for all $\epsilon>0$ and large $N$?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem greedySidon_go_singleton_two : (greedySidon.go {1} (by simp [IsSidon]) 2).val = 2

nodes.label: Let $A=\{a_1<\cdots<a_k\}$ be a finite set of positive integers and extend it to an infinite sequence $\overline{A}=\{a_1<a_2&#6
nodes.statement: Let $A=\{a_1<\cdots<a_k\}$ be a finite set of positive integers and extend it to an infinite sequence $\overline{A}=\{a_1<a_2<\cdots \}$ by defining $a_{n+1}$ for $n\geq k$ to be the least integer exceeding $a_n$ which is not of the form $a_i+a_j$ with $i,j\leq n$. Is it true that the sequence of differences $a_{m+1}-a_m$ is eventually periodic?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_341 :
    answer(sorry) ↔
      ∀ (a : ℕ → ℤ),
        (∀ᶠ n in atTop,
          IsLeast { x | a n < x ∧ x ∉ { a i + a j | (i ≤ n) (j ≤ n) } } (a (n + 1))) →
        let d := fun i ↦ a (i + 1) - a i
        ∃ p > 0, ∀ᶠ m in atTop, d (m + p) = d m

nodes.label: With $a_1=1$ and $a_2=2$ let $a_{n+1}$ for $n\geq 2$ be the least integer $>a_n$ which can be expressed uniquely as $a_i+a_j$ for $i&#60
nodes.statement: With $a_1=1$ and $a_2=2$ let $a_{n+1}$ for $n\geq 2$ be the least integer $>a_n$ which can be expressed uniquely as $a_i+a_j$ for $i<j\leq n$.What can be said about this sequence? Do infinitely many pairs $a,a+2$ occur? Does this sequence eventually have periodic differences? Is the density $0$?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_342.test.a0 : ∀ a : ℕ → ℕ, IsUlamSequence a → a 0 = 1

nodes.label: If $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is a multiset of integers such that\[\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert\gg N\]for all $N$ then must $A$ be subcomp
nodes.statement: If $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is a multiset of integers such that\[\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert\gg N\]for all $N$ then must $A$ be subcomplete? That is, must\[P(A) = \left\{\sum_{n\in B}n : B\subseteq A\textrm{ finite }\right\}\]contain an infinite arithmetic progression?

nodes.label: If $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is a set of integers such that\[\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert\gg N^{1/2}\]for all $N$ then must $A$ be subcom
nodes.statement: If $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is a set of integers such that\[\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert\gg N^{1/2}\]for all $N$ then must $A$ be subcomplete? That is, must\[P(A) = \left\{\sum_{n\in B}n : B\subseteq A\textrm{ finite }\right\}\]contain an infinite arithmetic progression?

nodes.label: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be a complete sequence, and define the threshold of completeness $T(A)$ to be the least integer $m$ such that al
nodes.statement: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be a complete sequence, and define the threshold of completeness $T(A)$ to be the least integer $m$ such that all $n\geq m$ are in\[P(A) = \left\{\sum_{n\in B}n : B\subseteq A\textrm{ finite }\right\}\](the existence of $T(A)$ is guaranteed by completeness). Is it true that there are infinitely many $k$ such that $T(n^k)>T(n^{k+1})$?

nodes.label: Let $A=\{1\leq a_1< a_2<\cdots\}$ be a set of integers such that $A\backslash B$ is complete for any finite subset $B$ and $A\backslash B$ i
nodes.statement: Let $A=\{1\leq a_1< a_2<\cdots\}$ be a set of integers such that $A\backslash B$ is complete for any finite subset $B$ and $A\backslash B$ is not complete for any infinite subset $B$. (Here 'complete' means all sufficiently large integers can be written as a sum of distinct members of the sequence.)Is it true that if $a_{n+1}/a_n \geq 1+\epsilon$ for some $\epsilon>0$ and all $n$ then\[\lim_n \frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}=\frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}?\]
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_346 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ {A : ℕ → ℕ}, IsLacunary A → IsAddStronglyCompleteNatSeq A →
    (∀ B : Set ℕ, B ⊆ range A → B.Infinite → ¬ IsAddComplete (range A \ B)) →
    Tendsto (fun n => A (n + 1) / (A n : ℝ)) atTop (𝓝 ((1 + √5) / 2))

nodes.label: Is there a sequence $A=\{a_1\leq a_2\leq \cdots\}$ of integers with\[\lim \frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}=2\]such that\[P(A')= \left\{\sum_{n\in B}n : B
nodes.statement: Is there a sequence $A=\{a_1\leq a_2\leq \cdots\}$ of integers with\[\lim \frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}=2\]such that\[P(A')= \left\{\sum_{n\in B}n : B\subseteq A'\textrm{ finite }\right\}\]has density $1$ for every cofinite subsequence $A'$ of $A$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_347 :
    answer(True) ↔ ∃ a : ℕ → ℕ, (Monotone a) ∧
      (Tendsto (fun n ↦ (a (n + 1) : ℝ) / (a n : ℝ)) atTop (𝓝 2)) ∧
      (∀ ι : ℕ → ℕ, (range ι)ᶜ.Finite → HasDensity (𝓟 (range (a ∘ ι))) 1)

nodes.label: For what values of $0\leq m<n$ is there a complete sequence $A=\{a_1\leq a_2\leq \cdots\}$ of integers such that $A$ remains complete after
nodes.statement: For what values of $0\leq m<n$ is there a complete sequence $A=\{a_1\leq a_2\leq \cdots\}$ of integers such that $A$ remains complete after removing any $m$ elements, but $A$ is not complete after removing any $n$ elements?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_348 :
    { (m, n) | (m) (n) (_ : m < n) (a : ℕ → ℕ) (_ : Monotone a)
      (_ : ∀ s, s.card = m → IsAddComplete (Set.range (Function.updateFinset a s 0)))
        (_ : ∀ t, t.card = n → ¬IsAddComplete (Set.range (Function.updateFinset a t 0))) } =
    answer(sorry)

nodes.label: For what values of $t,\alpha \in (0,\infty)$ is the sequence $\lfloor t\alpha^n\rfloor$ complete (that is, all sufficiently large integers a
nodes.statement: For what values of $t,\alpha \in (0,\infty)$ is the sequence $\lfloor t\alpha^n\rfloor$ complete (that is, all sufficiently large integers are the sum of distinct integers of the form $\lfloor t\alpha^n\rfloor$)?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_349 :
    {(t, α) | 0 < t ∧ 0 < α ∧ IsGoodPair t α} = answer(sorry)

nodes.label: If $A\subset\mathbb{N}$ is a finite set of integers which is dissociated (that is, all of the subset sums are distinct) then\[\sum_{n\in A}\
nodes.statement: If $A\subset\mathbb{N}$ is a finite set of integers which is dissociated (that is, all of the subset sums are distinct) then\[\sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n}<2.\]
nodes.formal_statement: theorem decidableDistinctSubsetSums_1_2 : DecidableDistinctSubsetSums {1, 2}

nodes.label: Let $p(x)\in \mathbb{Q}[x]$ with positive leading coefficient. Is it true that\[A=\{ p(n)+1/n : n\in \mathbb{N}\}\]is strongly complete, in
nodes.statement: Let $p(x)\in \mathbb{Q}[x]$ with positive leading coefficient. Is it true that\[A=\{ p(n)+1/n : n\in \mathbb{N}\}\]is strongly complete, in the sense that, for any finite set $B$,\[\left\{\sum_{n\in X}n : X\subseteq A\backslash B\textrm{ finite }\right\}\]contains all sufficiently large integers?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_351 :
    answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ P : ℚ[X], 0 < P.natDegree → 0 < P.leadingCoeff → HasCompleteImage P

nodes.label: Is there some $c>0$ such that every measurable $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ of measure $\geq c$ contains the vertices of a triangle of area
nodes.statement: Is there some $c>0$ such that every measurable $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ of measure $\geq c$ contains the vertices of a triangle of area 1?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_352 :
    answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ c > (0: ℝ), ∀ A : Set ℝ², MeasurableSet A → ℙ A ≥ c.toEReal
       → (∃ t : Affine.Triangle ℝ ℝ²,
           (∀ p : Fin 3, t.points p ∈ A) ∧
           EuclideanGeometry.triangle_area (t.points 0) (t.points 1) (t.points 2) = 1)

nodes.label: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ be a measurable set with infinite measure. Must $A$ contain the vertices of an isosceles trapezoid of area $1$
nodes.statement: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ be a measurable set with infinite measure. Must $A$ contain the vertices of an isosceles trapezoid of area $1$? What about an isosceles triangle, or a right-angled triangle, or a cyclic quadrilateral, or a polygon with congruent sides?

nodes.label: Let $\alpha,\beta\in \mathbb{R}_{>0}$ such that $\alpha/\beta$ is irrational. Is the multiset\[\{ \lfloor \alpha\rfloor,\lfloor 2\alpha\
nodes.statement: Let $\alpha,\beta\in \mathbb{R}_{>0}$ such that $\alpha/\beta$ is irrational. Is the multiset\[\{ \lfloor \alpha\rfloor,\lfloor 2\alpha\rfloor,\lfloor 4\alpha\rfloor,\ldots\}\cup \{ \lfloor \beta\rfloor,\lfloor 2\beta\rfloor,\lfloor 4\beta\rfloor,\ldots\}\]complete? That is, can all sufficiently large natural numbers $n$ be written as\[n=\sum_{s\in S}\lfloor 2^s\alpha\rfloor+\sum_{t\in T}\lfloor 2^t\beta\rfloor\]for some finite $S,T\subset \mathbb{N}$?What if $2$ is replaced by some $\gamma\in(1,2)$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_354.parts.i : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ᵉ (α > 0) (β > 0), Irrational (α / β) →
    IsAddCompleteNatSeq' (FloorMultiples.interleave α β 2)

nodes.label: Is there a lacunary sequence $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ (so that $A=\{a_1<a_2<\cdots\}$ and there exists some $\lambda>1$ such that $a_{n+1}/a_
nodes.statement: Is there a lacunary sequence $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ (so that $A=\{a_1<a_2<\cdots\}$ and there exists some $\lambda>1$ such that $a_{n+1}/a_n\geq \lambda$ for all $n\geq 1$) such that\[\left\{ \sum_{a\in A'}\frac{1}{a} : A'\subseteq A\textrm{ finite}\right\}\]contains all rationals in some open interval?

nodes.label: Is there some $c>0$ such that, for all sufficiently large $n$, there exist integers $a_1<\cdots<a_k\leq n$ such that there are a
nodes.statement: Is there some $c>0$ such that, for all sufficiently large $n$, there exist integers $a_1<\cdots<a_k\leq n$ such that there are at least $cn^2$ distinct integers of the form $\sum_{u\leq i\leq v}a_i$?

nodes.label: Let $1\leq a_1<\cdots <a_k\leq n$ be integers such that all sums of the shape $\sum_{u\leq i\leq v}a_i$ are distinct. Let $f(n)$ be
nodes.statement: Let $1\leq a_1<\cdots <a_k\leq n$ be integers such that all sums of the shape $\sum_{u\leq i\leq v}a_i$ are distinct. Let $f(n)$ be the maximal such $k$.How does $f(n)$ grow? Is $f(n)=o(n)$?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_357.parts.i : (fun n ↦ (f n : ℝ)) =o[atTop] (fun n ↦ (n : ℝ))

nodes.label: Let $A=\{a_1<\cdots\}$ be an infinite sequence of integers. Let $f(n)$ count the number of solutions to\[n=\sum_{u\leq i\leq v}a_i.\]Is
nodes.statement: Let $A=\{a_1<\cdots\}$ be an infinite sequence of integers. Let $f(n)$ count the number of solutions to\[n=\sum_{u\leq i\leq v}a_i.\]Is there such an $A$ for which $f(n)\to \infty$ as $n\to \infty$? Or even where $f(n)\geq 2$ for all large $n$?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem f_id : f id = fun n ↦ #{d ∈ n.divisors | Odd d}

nodes.label: Let $a_1<a_2<\cdots$ be an infinite sequence of integers such that $a_1=n$ and $a_{i+1}$ is the least integer which is not a sum of consecut
nodes.statement: Let $a_1<a_2<\cdots$ be an infinite sequence of integers such that $a_1=n$ and $a_{i+1}$ is the least integer which is not a sum of consecutive earlier $a_j$s. What can be said about the density of this sequence?In particular, in the case $n=1$, can one prove that $a_k/k\to \infty$ and $a_k/k^{1+c}\to 0$ for any $c>0$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_359.parts.i (A : ℕ → ℕ) (hA : IsGoodFor A 1) :
    atTop.Tendsto (fun k ↦ (A k : ℝ) / k) atTop

nodes.label: Let $f(n)$ be minimal such that $\{1,\ldots,n-1\}$ can be partitioned into $f(n)$ classes so that $n$ cannot be expressed as a sum of distin
nodes.statement: Let $f(n)$ be minimal such that $\{1,\ldots,n-1\}$ can be partitioned into $f(n)$ classes so that $n$ cannot be expressed as a sum of distinct elements from the same class. How fast does $f(n)$ grow?

nodes.label: Let $c>0$ and $n$ be some large integer. What is the size of the largest $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,\lfloor cn\rfloor\}$ such that $n$ is no
nodes.statement: Let $c>0$ and $n$ be some large integer. What is the size of the largest $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,\lfloor cn\rfloor\}$ such that $n$ is not a sum of a subset of $A$? Does this depend on $n$ in an irregular way?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_361.bigO
    (c : ℝ) (hc : 0 < c)
    (A : ℕ → ℕ)
    (hA : ∀ c n, A n = ((Finset.Icc 1 ⌊c * n⌋₊).powerset.filter
      (fun B ↦ n ≠ ∑ a ∈ B, a)).sup Finset.card) :
    (fun n ↦ (A n : ℝ)) =O[atTop] (answer(sorry) : ℕ → ℝ)

nodes.label: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be a finite set of size $N$. Is it true that, for any fixed $t$, there are\[\ll \frac{2^N}{N^{3/2}}\]many $S\sub
nodes.statement: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be a finite set of size $N$. Is it true that, for any fixed $t$, there are\[\ll \frac{2^N}{N^{3/2}}\]many $S\subseteq A$ such that $\sum_{n\in S}n=t$?If we further ask that $\lvert S\rvert=l$ (for any fixed $l$) then is the number of solutions\[\ll \frac{2^N}{N^2},\]with the implied constant independent of $l$ and $t$?

nodes.label: Is it true that there are only finitely many collections of disjoint intervals $I_1,\ldots,I_n$ of size $\lvert I_i\rvert \geq 4$ for $1\leq
nodes.statement: Is it true that there are only finitely many collections of disjoint intervals $I_1,\ldots,I_n$ of size $\lvert I_i\rvert \geq 4$ for $1\leq i\leq n$ such that\[\prod_{1\leq i\leq n}\prod_{m\in I_i}m\]is a square?

nodes.label: Are there any triples of consecutive positive integers all of which are powerful (i.e. if $p\mid n$ then $p^2\mid n$)?
nodes.statement: Are there any triples of consecutive positive integers all of which are powerful (i.e. if $p\mid n$ then $p^2\mid n$)?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_364 :
    ¬ ∃ (n : ℕ), Powerful n ∧ Powerful (n + 1) ∧ Powerful (n + 2)
nodes.closure_condition: An effective/unconditional abc-strength radical bound for the consecutive-triple configuration {n-1, n, n+1} all powerful — concretely: a lower bound rad(n(n-1)(n+1)) > C·(n^2)^{1-epsilon} (the abc inequality applied to the relation (n-1)(n+1) = n^2 - 1, where all three factors being powerful forces rad of each to be small) that holds for ALL n, not just finitely many. Equivalently, a complete solution of the simultaneous system 'n, n+1, n+2 each powerful', i.e. proving the only integral points on the associated family of generalized-Pell / superelliptic curves (a^2 b^3-type representations of three consecutive integers) are the empty set. The conditional path is closed (abc ⇒ finitely many triples; Mahler's Pell construction handles pairs); the missing input is removing the abc hypothesis OR a finite case-analysis that the deficiency/shape list of admissible triples is empty. Current partial results (Chan 2025 arXiv:2503.21485; She 2025 arXiv:2507.16828) only eliminate triples where n is a cube and n±1 have prescribed shapes p^3x^2 or p^2x^3; the general shapes remain open. Note: although the page tags this 'VERIFIABLE / could be proved with a finite example', a single triple would refute Erdős's NO-conjecture, but to confirm it (prove nonexistence) is NOT finite without an effective abc-type bound.

nodes.label: Do all pairs of consecutive powerful numbers $n$ and $n+1$ come from solutions to Pell equations? In other words, must either $n$ or $n+1$ b
nodes.statement: Do all pairs of consecutive powerful numbers $n$ and $n+1$ come from solutions to Pell equations? In other words, must either $n$ or $n+1$ be a square?Is the number of such $n\leq x$ bounded by $(\log x)^{O(1)}$?

nodes.label: Are there any $2$-full $n$ such that $n+1$ is $3$-full? That is, if $p\mid n$ then $p^2\mid n$ and if $p\mid n+1$ then $p^3\mid n+1$.
nodes.statement: Are there any $2$-full $n$ such that $n+1$ is $3$-full? That is, if $p\mid n$ then $p^2\mid n$ and if $p\mid n+1$ then $p^3\mid n+1$.
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_366 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ n > 0, (2).Full n ∧ (3).Full (n + 1)
nodes.closure_condition: Either (a) an explicit witness: a single integer n>0 with n 2-full (squarefull) and n+1 3-full (cubefull) — which would close the problem affirmatively by finite verification; or (b) a proof of non-existence. The current best partial input is the abc-conditional finiteness argument (rad(n)<=n^(1/2), rad(n+1)<=(n+1)^(1/3) applied to the triple (n,1,n+1) gives exponent (1-5e)/6 > 0, bounding n). The precise missing input is therefore EITHER an EFFECTIVE (unconditional, computable) upper bound K on any such n — which would reduce the problem to a finite search of n<=K — OR an unconditional rad-of-consecutive-powerful-pair lower bound of abc strength (rad(n(n+1)) >> (n(n+1))^c for some c>5/6) that forces finiteness without assuming abc. No 2-full/3-full consecutive pair exists for n<10^22 (OEIS A060355), and only the analogous 3-full/2-full pairs 8|9 and 12167|12168 are known, so the operative bottleneck is an effective/unconditional rad lower bound on consecutive powerful integers, not a witness search.

nodes.label: Are there infinitely many $n$ such that $\binom{2n}{n}$ is coprime to $105$?
nodes.statement: Are there infinitely many $n$ such that $\binom{2n}{n}$ is coprime to $105$?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_376 : answer(sorry) ↔ { (n : ℕ) | n.centralBinom.Coprime 105 }.Infinite
nodes.closure_condition:
A proof that the set of integers $n$ whose base-3 digits all lie in $\{0,1\}$, base-5 digits all lie in $\{0,1,2\}$, and base-7 digits all lie in $\{0,1,2,3\}$ (equivalently, by Kummer/Lucas, $n$ such that $\binom{2n}{n}$ is coprime to $105$, i.e. adding $n+n$ produces no carries in any of bases 3, 5, 7) is infinite — OR a proof it is finite. The missing input is a method handling the SIMULTANEOUS three-base digit restriction for the specific SMALL primes 3,5,7. Bloom-Croot [BlCr25] give the result only for sufficiently LARGE primes and only up to an $n^\epsilon$ factor (almost-all digits small), so the precise lemma needed is: upgrade the Bloom-Croot 'almost all digits $<p_i/2$' three-prime theorem to (a) ALL digits restricted (exact coprimality, removing the $n^\epsilon$ slop) and (b) the small primes $p_i\in\{3,5,7\}$ where the digit-restriction density per base is the borderline $\log_p\lceil p/2\rceil$ and the heuristic product of densities still predicts infinitely many but no carry-free construction or non-vanishing exponential-sum / transfer argument is available.">
<parameter name="closure_condition">duplicate

nodes.label: Let $2\leq k\leq n-2$. Can $\binom{n}{k}$ be the product of consecutive primes infinitely often? For example\[\binom{21}{2}=2\cdot 3\cdot 5\
nodes.statement: Let $2\leq k\leq n-2$. Can $\binom{n}{k}$ be the product of consecutive primes infinitely often? For example\[\binom{21}{2}=2\cdot 3\cdot 5\cdot 7.\]
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_386 :
    answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ k ≥ 2, ∃ᶠ n in .atTop,
      k ≤ n - 2 ∧ ∃ p q : ℕ, n.choose k = ∏ i ∈ .Ico p q, nth Nat.Prime i
nodes.closure_condition: The bottleneck is the infinitude question for a single fixed k (the website's primary form is the existential: some k≥2 with binom(n,k) a product of consecutive primes for infinitely many n; the k=2 case binom(n,2)=n(n-1)/2 is the canonical hard instance). To CLOSE it one needs EITHER (a) a construction/parametrization producing an infinite family of n with binom(n,k)=∏_{i∈[p,q)} prime(i) — equivalently, solving the multiplicative S-unit-style Diophantine system forcing a primorial-window factorization n(n-1)/2 = product of the consecutive primes in an interval [P,Q] with NO prime outside that interval dividing it AND each such prime to multiplicity exactly 1 — OR (b) a finiteness theorem showing only finitely many such n exist. The precise missing input is an effective bound on n for which binom(n,2)=n(n-1)/2 is squarefree-and-consecutive-prime-supported: a Diophantine finiteness result (Subspace/S-unit-equation strength) ruling out arbitrarily large n where the prime factors of n(n-1) form an unbroken initial-or-interval segment of the primes each with the right exponent. Neither the construction nor the finiteness bound exists; the known list 4,6,15,21,715 (A280992) is conjecturally complete for k=2 but not proven complete.

nodes.label: Is there an absolute constant $c>0$ such that, for all $1\leq k< n$, the binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{k}$ has a divisor in $(cn,n
nodes.statement: Is there an absolute constant $c>0$ such that, for all $1\leq k< n$, the binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{k}$ has a divisor in $(cn,n]$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_387 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ c : ℝ, 0 < c ∧ ∀ n k : ℕ, 1 ≤ k → k < n →
    ∃ d : ℕ, (d : ℝ) ∈ Set.Ioc (c * n) n ∧ d ∣ n.choose k
nodes.closure_condition:
An UNCONDITIONAL proof of the covering theorem in the Pratt-Bui-Zaharescu reduction (arXiv, May 21 2026), which currently holds only under GRH. The problem reduces (along lines Tao suggested) to (i) a covering problem and (ii) a divisor problem. The divisor problem is already settled unconditionally via sieve theory plus exponential-sum bounds. The covering problem (constructing, for suitable non-prime-power k, infinitely many n such that every integer in (cn, n] fails to divide C(n,k)) was only achievable assuming GRH, needed to control prime and zero distribution in the covering construction. Closing the problem with the expected NEGATIVE answer (NO absolute c>0 works) requires removing GRH from this covering step. As of May 29 2026 a candidate unconditional covering argument (Chojecki, GPT-5.5-assisted) exists but is unverified and resembles an earlier note still being refined.</closure_condition>
<parameter name="notes">Lean file at /Users/williamblair/personal/formal-conjectures/FormalConjectures/ErdosProblems/387.lean encodes the main question (research open), the Schinzel variant (open), Guy c<1 variant (open), plus two SOLVED facts: easy [n/k,n] divisor existence (gcd argument) and the Schinzel-Erdos counterexample n=99215,k=15 to Erdos original (n-k,n] conjecture (native_decide). Closure depends on a shared lemma cluster (unconditional covering for non-prime-power k) likely overlapping Schinzel B34 and Faulkner-type prime-factor results. GRH-removal is the exact leverage point. Difficulty 4: concrete near-complete program with one identified conditional gap. Website lists OPEN (edit 23 Jan 2026) but forum activity through 29 May 2026 shows live resolution.</notes>
</invoke>

nodes.statement: For which integers $a\geq 1$ and primes $p$ is there a finite upper bound on those $k$ such that there are $a=a_1<\cdots<a_n$ with\[p^k \mid (a_1!+\cdots+a_n!)?\]If $f(a,p)$ is the greatest such $k$, how does this function behave?Is there a prime $p$ and an infinite sequence $a_1<a_2<\cdots$ such that if $p^{m_k}$ is the highest power of $p$ dividing $\sum_{i\leq k}a_i!$ then $m_k\to \infty$?

nodes.statement: Let $p$ be an odd prime. Is it true that the equation\[(p-1)!+a^{p-1}=p^k\]has only finitely many solutions?

nodes.label: Is it true that there are only finitely many powers of $2$ which have only the digits $0$ and $1$ when written in base $3$?
nodes.statement: Is it true that there are only finitely many powers of $2$ which have only the digits $0$ and $1$ when written in base $3$?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_406 : answer(sorry) ↔ { n | n.isPowerOfTwo ∧ Nat.digits 3 n ⊆ [0, 1] }.Finite

nodes.label: Let $w(n)$ count the number of solutions to\[n=2^a+3^b+2^c3^d\]with $a,b,c,d\geq 0$ integers. Is it true that $w(n)$ is bounded by some abso
nodes.statement: Let $w(n)$ count the number of solutions to\[n=2^a+3^b+2^c3^d\]with $a,b,c,d\geq 0$ integers. Is it true that $w(n)$ is bounded by some absolute constant?

nodes.label: Let $\phi(n)$ be the Euler totient function and $\phi_k(n)$ be the iterated $\phi$ function, so that $\phi_1(n)=\phi(n)$ and $\phi_k(n)=\phi
nodes.statement: Let $\phi(n)$ be the Euler totient function and $\phi_k(n)$ be the iterated $\phi$ function, so that $\phi_1(n)=\phi(n)$ and $\phi_k(n)=\phi(\phi_{k-1}(n))$. Let\[f(n) = \min \{ k : \phi_k(n)=1\}.\]Does $f(n)/\log n$ have a distribution function? Is $f(n)/\log n$ almost always constant? What can be said about the largest prime factor of $\phi_k(n)$ when, say, $k=\log\log n$?

nodes.label: How many iterations of $n\mapsto \phi(n)+1$ are needed before a prime is reached? Can infinitely many $n$ reach the same prime? What is the
nodes.statement: How many iterations of $n\mapsto \phi(n)+1$ are needed before a prime is reached? Can infinitely many $n$ reach the same prime? What is the density of $n$ which reach any fixed prime?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_409.parts.i (n : ℕ) (hn : 0 < n) :
    IsLeast { i | (φ · + 1)^[i] n |>.Prime } answer(sorry)

nodes.label: Let $\sigma_1(n)=\sigma(n)$, the sum of divisors function, and $\sigma_k(n)=\sigma(\sigma_{k-1}(n))$. Is it true that for all $n\geq 2$\[\li
nodes.statement: Let $\sigma_1(n)=\sigma(n)$, the sum of divisors function, and $\sigma_k(n)=\sigma(\sigma_{k-1}(n))$. Is it true that for all $n\geq 2$\[\lim_{k\to \infty} \sigma_k(n)^{1/k}=\infty?\]
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_410 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ n > 1,
    Tendsto (fun k : ℕ ↦ ((sigma 1)^[k] n : ℝ) ^ (1 / (k : ℝ))) atTop atTop
nodes.closure_condition: A proof that for every n>1 the per-step abundancy ratio σ(σ_{k}(n))/σ_k(n) is unbounded along the iteration — i.e. that the small-prime support (the count, weighted by log, of small prime factors) of the iterates σ_k(n) grows without bound with k, so that σ_k(n)^{1/k} = exp((1/k)Σ_{j<k} log(σ_{j+1}/σ_j)) → ∞. Concretely the missing input is a lower bound on the normal number/size of small prime divisors of the iterated σ-values forcing Σ_{j<k} log(σ(σ_j(n))/σ_j(n)) to grow superlinearly in k; equivalently, ruling out any starting n whose σ-orbit settles into bounded abundancy (bounded ω over small primes). No finite computation can decide it. Refutation would require exhibiting an n whose orbit keeps abundancy σ_k(n)/σ_{k-1}(n) bounded (limit finite).

nodes.label: Let $g_1=g(n)=n+\phi(n)$ and $g_k(n)=g(g_{k-1}(n))$. For which $n$ and $r$ is it true that $g_{k+r}(n)=2g_k(n)$ for all large $k$?
nodes.statement: Let $g_1=g(n)=n+\phi(n)$ and $g_k(n)=g(g_{k-1}(n))$. For which $n$ and $r$ is it true that $g_{k+r}(n)=2g_k(n)$ for all large $k$?

nodes.label: Let $\sigma_1(n)=\sigma(n)$, the sum of divisors function, and $\sigma_k(n)=\sigma(\sigma_{k-1}(n))$. Is it true that, for every $m,n\geq 2$
nodes.statement: Let $\sigma_1(n)=\sigma(n)$, the sum of divisors function, and $\sigma_k(n)=\sigma(\sigma_{k-1}(n))$. Is it true that, for every $m,n\geq 2$, there exist some $i,j$ such that $\sigma_i(m)=\sigma_j(n)$?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_412 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ᵉ (m ≥ 2) (n ≥ 2), ∃ i j, (σ 1)^[i] m = (σ 1)^[j] n

nodes.label: Let $\omega(n)$ count the number of distinct primes dividing $n$. Are there infinitely many $n$ such that, for all $m<n$, we have $m+\omega(
nodes.statement: Let $\omega(n)$ count the number of distinct primes dividing $n$. Are there infinitely many $n$ such that, for all $m<n$, we have $m+\omega(m) \leq n$?Can one show that there exists an $\epsilon>0$ such that there are infinitely many $n$ where $m+\epsilon \omega(m)\leq n$ for all $m<n$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_413.parts.i :
    answer(sorry) ↔ { n | IsBarrier (fun m => ω m) n }.Infinite

nodes.label: Let $h_1(n)=h(n)=n+\tau(n)$ (where $\tau(n)$ counts the number of divisors of $n$) and $h_k(n)=h(h_{k-1}(n))$. Is it true, for any $m,n$, th
nodes.statement: Let $h_1(n)=h(n)=n+\tau(n)$ (where $\tau(n)$ counts the number of divisors of $n$) and $h_k(n)=h(h_{k-1}(n))$. Is it true, for any $m,n$, there exist $i$ and $j$ such that $h_i(m)=h_j(n)$?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_414 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ᵉ  (m > 0) (n > 0), ∃ i j, h^[i] m = h^[j] n

nodes.label: For any $n$ let $F(n)$ be the largest $k$ such that any of the $k!$ possible ordering patterns appears in some sequence of $\phi(m+1),\ldots
nodes.statement: For any $n$ let $F(n)$ be the largest $k$ such that any of the $k!$ possible ordering patterns appears in some sequence of $\phi(m+1),\ldots,\phi(m+k)$ with $m+k\leq n$. Is it true that\[F(n)=(c+o(1))\log\log\log n\]for some constant $c$? Is the first pattern which fails to appear always\[\phi(m+1)>\phi(m+2)>\cdots >\phi(m+k)?\]Is it true that the 'natural' ordering which mimics what happens to $\phi(1),\ldots,\phi(k)$ is the most likely to appear?

nodes.label: Let $V(x)$ count the number of $n\leq x$ such that $\phi(m)=n$ is solvable. Does $V(2x)/V(x)\to 2$? Is there an asymptotic formula for $V(x)
nodes.statement: Let $V(x)$ count the number of $n\leq x$ such that $\phi(m)=n$ is solvable. Does $V(2x)/V(x)\to 2$? Is there an asymptotic formula for $V(x)$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_416.parts.i :
    Filter.Tendsto (fun x => (V (2 * x) / V (x))) Filter.atTop (𝓝 2)

nodes.label: Let\[V'(x)=\#\{\phi(m) : 1\leq m\leq x\}\]and\[V(x)=\#\{\phi(m) \leq x : 1\leq m\}.\]Does $\lim V(x)/V'(x)$ exist? Is it $>1$?
nodes.statement: Let\[V'(x)=\#\{\phi(m) : 1\leq m\leq x\}\]and\[V(x)=\#\{\phi(m) \leq x : 1\leq m\}.\]Does $\lim V(x)/V'(x)$ exist? Is it $>1$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_417.parts.i :
    answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ L : ℝ, Tendsto (fun x ↦
      ((totient '' { m | 1 ≤ m ∧ (m : ℝ) ≤ x }).ncard : ℝ) /
      ({ k | k ∈ range totient ∧ (k : ℝ) ≤ x }.ncard : ℝ))
      atTop (𝓝 L)

nodes.label: Are there infinitely many positive integers not of the form $n-\phi(n)$?
nodes.statement: Are there infinitely many positive integers not of the form $n-\phi(n)$?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_418 : answer(True) ↔ { (n - n.totient : ℕ) | n }ᶜ.Infinite

nodes.label: If $\tau(n)$ counts the number of divisors of $n$, then what is the set of limit points of\[\frac{\tau((n+1)!)}{\tau(n!)}?\]
nodes.statement: If $\tau(n)$ counts the number of divisors of $n$, then what is the set of limit points of\[\frac{\tau((n+1)!)}{\tau(n!)}?\]

nodes.label: If $\tau(n)$ counts the number of divisors of $n$ then let\[F(f,n)=\frac{\tau((n+\lfloor f(n)\rfloor)!)}{\tau(n!)}.\]Is it true that\[\lim_{
nodes.statement: If $\tau(n)$ counts the number of divisors of $n$ then let\[F(f,n)=\frac{\tau((n+\lfloor f(n)\rfloor)!)}{\tau(n!)}.\]Is it true that\[\lim_{n\to \infty}F((\log n)^C,n)=\infty\]for large $C$? Is it true that $F(\log n,n)$ is everywhere dense in $(1,\infty)$? More generally, if $f(n)\leq \log n$ is a monotonic function such that $f(n)\to \infty$ as $n\to \infty$, then is $F(f,n)$ everywhere dense?

nodes.label: Is there a sequence $1\leq d_1<d_2<\cdots$ with density $1$ such that all products $\prod_{u\leq i\leq v}d_i$ are distinct?
nodes.statement: Is there a sequence $1\leq d_1<d_2<\cdots$ with density $1$ such that all products $\prod_{u\leq i\leq v}d_i$ are distinct?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_421 : answer(sorry) ↔
    ∃ (d : ℕ → ℕ), StrictMono d ∧ 1 ≤ d 0 ∧ HasDensity (Set.range d) 1 ∧
    {(u, v) : ℕ × ℕ | u ≤ v}.InjOn fun (u, v) => ∏ i ∈ Finset.Icc u v, d i

nodes.label: Let $f(1)=f(2)=1$ and for $n>2$\[f(n) = f(n-f(n-1))+f(n-f(n-2)).\]Does $f(n)$ miss infinitely many integers? What is its behaviour?
nodes.statement: Let $f(1)=f(2)=1$ and for $n>2$\[f(n) = f(n-f(n-1))+f(n-f(n-2)).\]Does $f(n)$ miss infinitely many integers? What is its behaviour?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_422 : answer(sorry) ↔ Set.Infinite {n | ∀ x, f x ≠ n}

nodes.label: Let $a_1=1$ and $a_2=2$ and for $k\geq 3$ choose $a_k$ to be the least integer $>a_{k-1}$ which is the sum of at least two consecutive t
nodes.statement: Let $a_1=1$ and $a_2=2$ and for $k\geq 3$ choose $a_k$ to be the least integer $>a_{k-1}$ which is the sum of at least two consecutive terms of the sequence. What is the asymptotic behaviour of this sequence?

nodes.label: Let $a_1=2$ and $a_2=3$ and continue the sequence by appending to $a_1,\ldots,a_n$ all possible values of $a_ia_j-1$ with $i\neq j$. Is it t
nodes.statement: Let $a_1=2$ and $a_2=3$ and continue the sequence by appending to $a_1,\ldots,a_n$ all possible values of $a_ia_j-1$ with $i\neq j$. Is it true that the set of integers which eventually appear has positive density?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_424 : answer(sorry) ↔ generatedSet.HasPosDensity

nodes.label: Let $F(n)$ be the maximum possible size of a subset $A\subseteq\{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that the products $ab$ are distinct for all $a<b$. I
nodes.statement: Let $F(n)$ be the maximum possible size of a subset $A\subseteq\{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that the products $ab$ are distinct for all $a<b$. Is there a constant $c$ such that\[F(n)=\pi(n)+(c+o(1))n^{3/4}(\log n)^{-3/2}?\]If $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}$ is such that all products $a_1\cdots a_r$ are distinct for $a_1<\cdots <a_r$ then is it true that\[\lvert A\rvert \leq \pi(n)+O(n^{\frac{r+1}{2r}})?\]

nodes.label: We say $H$ is a unique subgraph of $G$ if there is exactly one way to find $H$ as a subgraph (not necessarily induced) of $G$. Is there a gr
nodes.statement: We say $H$ is a unique subgraph of $G$ if there is exactly one way to find $H$ as a subgraph (not necessarily induced) of $G$. Is there a graph on $n$ vertices with\[\gg \frac{2^{\binom{n}{2}}}{n!}\]many distinct unique subgraphs?

nodes.label: Is it true that, for every $n$ and $d$, there exists $k$ such that\[d \mid p_{n+1}+\cdots+p_{n+k},\]where $p_r$ denotes the $r$th prime?
nodes.statement: Is it true that, for every $n$ and $d$, there exists $k$ such that\[d \mid p_{n+1}+\cdots+p_{n+k},\]where $p_r$ denotes the $r$th prime?
nodes.formal_statement: theorem erdos_427 : answer(True) ↔ erdos427

nodes.label: Is there a set $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ such that, for infinitely many $n$, all of $n-a$ are prime for all $a\in A$ with $0<a<n$ and\[\liminf
nodes.statement: Is there a set $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ such that, for infinitely many $n$, all of $n-a$ are prime for all $a\in A$ with $0<a<n$ and\[\liminf\frac{\lvert A\cap [1,x]\rvert}{\pi(x)}>0?\]
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_428 :
    answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ A : Set ℕ,
      (∃ᶠ n in atTop, ∀ a ∈ A, 0 < a → a < n → (n - a).Prime) ∧
      liminf (fun n ↦ primeDensityRatio A n) atTop > 0

nodes.label: Is it true that, if $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is sparse enough and does not cover all residue classes modulo $p$ for any prime $p$, then there
nodes.statement: Is it true that, if $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is sparse enough and does not cover all residue classes modulo $p$ for any prime $p$, then there exists some $n$ such that $n+a$ is prime for all $a\in A$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_1209.parts.i :
    answer(False) ↔
      ∃ f : ℕ → ℕ, ∀ a : ℕ → ℕ, StrictMono a → (∀ k, f k ≤ a k) →
        (∃ n, ∀ k, (n + a k).Prime) →
        {n | ∀ k, (n + a k).Prime}.Infinite

nodes.label: Fix some integer $n$ and define a decreasing sequence in $[1,n)$ by $a_1=n-1$ and, for $k\geq 2$, letting $a_k$ be the greatest integer in $
nodes.statement: Fix some integer $n$ and define a decreasing sequence in $[1,n)$ by $a_1=n-1$ and, for $k\geq 2$, letting $a_k$ be the greatest integer in $[1,a_{k-1})$ such that all of the prime factors of $a_k$ are $>n-a_k$.Is it true that, for sufficiently large $n$, not all of this sequence can be prime?

nodes.label: Are there two infinite sets $A$ and $B$ such that $A+B$ agrees with the set of prime numbers up to finitely many exceptions?
nodes.statement: Are there two infinite sets $A$ and $B$ such that $A+B$ agrees with the set of prime numbers up to finitely many exceptions?

nodes.label: Let $A,B\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be two infinite sets. How dense can $A+B$ be if all elements of $A+B$ are pairwise relatively prime?
nodes.statement: Let $A,B\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be two infinite sets. How dense can $A+B$ be if all elements of $A+B$ are pairwise relatively prime?

nodes.label: If $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ is a finite set then let $G(A)$ denote the greatest integer which is not expressible as a finite sum of elements fr
nodes.statement: If $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ is a finite set then let $G(A)$ denote the greatest integer which is not expressible as a finite sum of elements from $A$ (with repetitions allowed). Let\[g(k,n)=\max G(A)\]where the maximum is taken over all $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}$ of size $\lvert A\rvert=k$ which has no common divisor. Is it true that\[g(k,n)\sim \frac{n^2}{k-1}?\]

nodes.label: Let $k\leq n$. What choice of $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}$ (with $\mathrm{gcd}(A)=1$) of size $\lvert A\rvert=k$ maximises the number of inte
nodes.statement: Let $k\leq n$. What choice of $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}$ (with $\mathrm{gcd}(A)=1$) of size $\lvert A\rvert=k$ maximises the number of integers not representable as the sum of finitely many elements from $A$ (with repetitions allowed)? Is it $\{n,n-1,\ldots,n-k+1\}$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_434.parts.i (n k : ℕ) (hn : 1 ≤ n) (hk : 1 ≤ k) (h : k ≤ n) :
    IsGreatest
      { Nat.NcardUnrepresentable S | (S : Finset ℕ) (_ : S ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 n)
        (_ : #S = k) (_ : S.gcd id = 1) }
      (Nat.NcardUnrepresentable <| answer(sorry))

nodes.label: Let $n\in\mathbb{N}$ with $n\neq p^k$ for any prime $p$ and $k\geq 0$. What is the largest integer not of the form\[\sum_{1\leq i<n}c_i\
nodes.statement: Let $n\in\mathbb{N}$ with $n\neq p^k$ for any prime $p$ and $k\geq 0$. What is the largest integer not of the form\[\sum_{1\leq i<n}c_i\binom{n}{i}\]where the $c_i\geq 0$ are integers?

nodes.label: If $p$ is a prime and $k,m\geq 2$ then let $r(k,m,p)$ be the minimal $r$ such that $r,r+1,\ldots,r+m-1$ are all $k$th power residues modulo
nodes.statement: If $p$ is a prime and $k,m\geq 2$ then let $r(k,m,p)$ be the minimal $r$ such that $r,r+1,\ldots,r+m-1$ are all $k$th power residues modulo $p$. Let\[\Lambda(k,m)=\limsup_{p\to \infty} r(k,m,p).\]Is it true that $\Lambda(k,2)$ is finite for all $k$? Is $\Lambda(k,3)$ finite for all odd $k$? How large are they?

nodes.label: Let $1\leq a_1<\cdots<a_k\leq x$. How many of the partial products $a_1,a_1a_2,\ldots,a_1\cdots a_k$ can be squares? Is it true that, for an
nodes.statement: Let $1\leq a_1<\cdots<a_k\leq x$. How many of the partial products $a_1,a_1a_2,\ldots,a_1\cdots a_k$ can be squares? Is it true that, for any $\epsilon>0$, there can be more than $x^{1-\epsilon}$ squares?

nodes.label: How large can $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ be if $A+A$ contains no square numbers?
nodes.statement: How large can $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ be if $A+A$ contains no square numbers?

nodes.label: Is it true that, in any finite colouring of the integers, there must be two integers $x\neq y$ of the same colour such that $x+y$ is a squar
nodes.statement: Is it true that, in any finite colouring of the integers, there must be two integers $x\neq y$ of the same colour such that $x+y$ is a square? What about a $k$th power?

nodes.label: Let $A=\{a_1<a_2<\cdots\}\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be infinite and let $A(x)$ count the number of indices for which $\mathrm{lcm}(a_i,a_
nodes.statement: Let $A=\{a_1<a_2<\cdots\}\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be infinite and let $A(x)$ count the number of indices for which $\mathrm{lcm}(a_i,a_{i+1})\leq x$. Is it true that $A(x) \ll x^{1/2}$? How large can\[\liminf \frac{A(x)}{x^{1/2}}\]be?

nodes.label: Let $N\geq 1$. What is the size of the largest $A\subset \{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that $[a,b]\leq N$ for all $a,b\in A$, where $[a,b]$ is the le
nodes.statement: Let $N\geq 1$. What is the size of the largest $A\subset \{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that $[a,b]\leq N$ for all $a,b\in A$, where $[a,b]$ is the least common multiple of $a$ and $b$?Is it attained by choosing all integers in $[1,(N/2)^{1/2}]$ together with all even integers in $[(N/2)^{1/2},(2N)^{1/2}]$?

nodes.label: Is it true that if $A\subseteq\mathbb{N}$ is such that\[\frac{1}{\log\log x}\sum_{n\in A\cap [1,x)}\frac{1}{n}\to \infty\]then\[\left(\sum_{
nodes.statement: Is it true that if $A\subseteq\mathbb{N}$ is such that\[\frac{1}{\log\log x}\sum_{n\in A\cap [1,x)}\frac{1}{n}\to \infty\]then\[\left(\sum_{n\in A\cap [1,x)}\frac{1}{n}\right)^{-2} \sum_{\substack{a,b\in A\cap (1,x]\\ a<b}}\frac{1}{\mathrm{lcm}(a,b)}\to \infty?\]
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_442 : answer(False) ↔ ∀ (A : Set ℕ),
    Tendsto (fun (x : ℝ) =>
      1 / x.maxLogOne.maxLogOne * ∑ n ∈ (A ∩ Icc 1 ⌊x⌋₊ : Set ℕ), (1 : ℝ) / n) atTop atTop →
    Tendsto (fun (x : ℝ) => 1 / (∑ n ∈ (A ∩ Icc 1 ⌊x⌋₊ : Set ℕ), (1 : ℝ) / n) ^ 2 *
      ∑ nm ∈ A.bddProdUpper x, (1 : ℝ) / nm.1.lcm nm.2) atTop atTop

nodes.label: Let $m,n\geq 1$. What is\[\# \{ k(m-k) : 1\leq k\leq m/2\} \cap \{ l(n-l) : 1\leq l\leq n/2\}?\]Can it be arbitrarily large? Is it $\leq (mn
nodes.statement: Let $m,n\geq 1$. What is\[\# \{ k(m-k) : 1\leq k\leq m/2\} \cap \{ l(n-l) : 1\leq l\leq n/2\}?\]Can it be arbitrarily large? Is it $\leq (mn)^{o(1)}$ for all sufficiently large $m,n$?

nodes.label: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{N}$ be infinite and $d_A(n)$ count the number of $a\in A$ which divide $n$. Is it true that, for every $k$,\[\limsup_
nodes.statement: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{N}$ be infinite and $d_A(n)$ count the number of $a\in A$ which divide $n$. Is it true that, for every $k$,\[\limsup_{x\to \infty} \frac{\max_{n<x}d_A(n)}{\left(\sum_{n\in A\cap[1,x)}\frac{1}{n}\right)^k}=\infty?\]

nodes.label: Is it true that, for any $c>1/2$, if $p$ is a sufficiently large prime then, for any $n\geq 0$, there exist $a,b\in(n,n+p^c)$ such that
nodes.statement: Is it true that, for any $c>1/2$, if $p$ is a sufficiently large prime then, for any $n\geq 0$, there exist $a,b\in(n,n+p^c)$ such that $ab\equiv 1\pmod{p}$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_445 :
    answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ c : ℝ, c > 1 / 2 →
      ∀ᶠ p : ℕ in atTop, p.Prime → ∀ n : ℕ, Erdos445Prop c p n

nodes.label: Let $\delta(n)$ denote the density of integers which are divisible by some integer in $(n,2n)$. What is the growth rate of $\delta(n)$?If $\
nodes.statement: Let $\delta(n)$ denote the density of integers which are divisible by some integer in $(n,2n)$. What is the growth rate of $\delta(n)$?If $\delta_1(n)$ is the density of integers which have exactly one divisor in $(n,2n)$ then is it true that $\delta_1(n)=o(\delta(n))$?

nodes.label: How large can a union-free collection $\mathcal{F}$ of subsets of $[n]$ be? By union-free we mean there are no solutions to $A\cup B=C$ with
nodes.statement: How large can a union-free collection $\mathcal{F}$ of subsets of $[n]$ be? By union-free we mean there are no solutions to $A\cup B=C$ with distinct $A,B,C\in \mathcal{F}$. Must $\lvert \mathcal{F}\rvert =o(2^n)$? Perhaps even\[\lvert \mathcal{F}\rvert <(1+o(1))\binom{n}{\lfloor n/2\rfloor}?\]

nodes.label: Let $\tau(n)$ count the divisors of $n$ and $\tau^+(n)$ count the number of $k$ such that $n$ has a divisor in $[2^k,2^{k+1})$. Is it true t
nodes.statement: Let $\tau(n)$ count the divisors of $n$ and $\tau^+(n)$ count the number of $k$ such that $n$ has a divisor in $[2^k,2^{k+1})$. Is it true that, for all $\epsilon>0$,\[\tau^+(n) < \epsilon \tau(n)\]for almost all $n$?

nodes.label: Let $r(n)$ count the number of $d_1,d_2$ such that $d_1\mid n$ and $d_2\mid n$ and $d_1<d_2<2d_1$. Is it true that, for every $\epsilon>0$,\
nodes.statement: Let $r(n)$ count the number of $d_1,d_2$ such that $d_1\mid n$ and $d_2\mid n$ and $d_1<d_2<2d_1$. Is it true that, for every $\epsilon>0$,\[r(n) < \epsilon \tau(n)\]for almost all $n$, where $\tau(n)$ is the number of divisors of $n$?

nodes.label: Let $q_1<q_2<\cdots$ be a sequence of primes such that\[q_{n+1}-q_n\geq q_n-q_{n-1}.\]Must\[\lim_n \frac{q_n}{n^2}=\infty?\]

theorem erdos_455: answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ q : ℕ → ℕ, StrictMono q →
    (∀ n, (q n).Prime ∧ q (n + 2) - q (n + 1) ≥ q (n + 1) - q n) →
    Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => (q n : ℝ) / n ^ 2) atTop atTop

nodes.label: Let $p_n$ be the smallest prime $\equiv 1\pmod{n}$ and let $m_n$ be the smallest integer such that $n\mid \phi(m_n)$.Is it true that $m_n&#6
nodes.statement: Let $p_n$ be the smallest prime $\equiv 1\pmod{n}$ and let $m_n$ be the smallest integer such that $n\mid \phi(m_n)$.Is it true that $m_n<p_n$ for almost all $n$? Does $p_n/m_n\to \infty$ for almost all $n$? Are there infinitely many primes $p$ such that $p-1$ is the only $n$ for which $m_n=p$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_456.parts.i :
    answer(sorry) ↔
      Tendsto (fun N ↦ (count { n | m n < p n } N : ℝ) / (N : ℝ)) atTop (𝓝 1)

nodes.label: Is there some $\epsilon>0$ such that there are infinitely many $n$ where all primes $p\leq (2+\epsilon)\log n$ divide\[\prod_{1\leq i\le
nodes.statement: Is there some $\epsilon>0$ such that there are infinitely many $n$ where all primes $p\leq (2+\epsilon)\log n$ divide\[\prod_{1\leq i\leq \log n}(n+i)?\]
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_457 : answer(True) ↔ ∃ ε > (0 : ℝ),
    { (n : ℕ) | ∀ (p : ℕ), p ≤ (2 + ε) * Real.log n → p.Prime →
      p ∣ ∏ i ∈ Finset.Icc 1 ⌊Real.log n⌋₊, (n + i) }.Infinite

nodes.statement: Let $[1,\ldots,n]$ denote the least common multiple of $\{1,\ldots,n\}$. Is it true that, for all $k\geq 1$,\[[1,\ldots,p_{k+1}-1]< p_k[1,\ldots,p_k]?\]

theorem erdos_458 :
    answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ k : ℕ, lcm_upto ((k + 1).nth Prime - 1)

nodes.statement: Let $a_0=0$ and $a_1=1$, and in general define $a_k$ to be the least integer $>a_{k-1}$ for which $(n-a_k,n-a_i)=1$ for all $0\leq i<k$. Does\[\sum_{0<a_i< n}\frac{1}{a_i}\to \infty\]as $n\to \infty$? What about if we restrict the sum to those $i$ such that $n-a_j$ is divisible by some prime $\leq a_j$, or the complement of such $i$?

Let $f(n,t)$ count the number of distinct possible values for $s_t(m)$ for $m\in [n+1,n+t]$. Is it true that\[f(n,t)\gg t\](uniformly, for all $t$ and $n$)?

nodes.label: Let $p(n)$ denote the least prime factor of $n$. There is a constant $c>0$ such that\[\sum_{\substack{n<x\\ n\textrm{ not prime}}}\frac{
nodes.statement: Let $p(n)$ denote the least prime factor of $n$. There is a constant $c>0$ such that\[\sum_{\substack{n<x\\ n\textrm{ not prime}}}\frac{p(n)}{n}\sim c\frac{x^{1/2}}{(\log x)^2}.\]Is it true that there exists a constant $C>0$ such that\[\sum_{x\leq n\leq x+Cx^{1/2}(\log x)^2}\frac{p(n)}{n} \gg 1\]for all large $x$?

nodes.label: Is there a function $f$ with $f(n)\to \infty$ as $n\to \infty$ such that, for all large $n$, there is a composite number $m$ such that\[n+f(
nodes.statement: Is there a function $f$ with $f(n)\to \infty$ as $n\to \infty$ such that, for all large $n$, there is a composite number $m$ such that\[n+f(n)<m<n+p(m)?\](Here $p(m)$ is the least prime factor of $m$.)
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_463 : answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ (f : ℕ → ℕ) (_ : Tendsto f atTop atTop),
    ∀ᶠ n in atTop,
      ∃ m, m.Composite ∧
        n + f n < m ∧ m < n + m.minFac
nodes.closure_condition: The bottleneck is a quantitative result on the local distribution of the function m - p(m) (m composite, p(m) = least prime factor) in windows just above n. Concretely: one must show (for the YES answer) that for every large n there exists a composite m with m - p(m) just below n and a growing margin g(n) -> infinity, i.e. that integers m near n with LARGE least prime factor p(m) (m a product of two primes both near sqrt(m), so p(m) ≍ sqrt(m)) are dense enough that the values m - p(m) cover the interval (n - f(n), n) leaving the required gap; equivalently, control the gaps in the value-set { m - p(m) }. This is governed by Erdős's companion conjecture F(n) = min_{m>n}(m - p(m)) with n - F(n) ~ c·n^{1/2}, which depends on counting semiprimes p·q with p,q ≈ sqrt(m) in short intervals and the spacing of consecutive such m — itself dependent on prime-distribution-in-short-intervals / Selberg-type sieve estimates that are conditional on 'plausible conjectures on primes' (as Erdős-Eggleton-Selfridge note for the twin problem #385). No finite computation can settle it; the precise missing input is an unconditional short-interval count of integers with least prime factor ≍ sqrt(m) sharp enough to force m - p(m) to leave a divergent gap below n.

nodes.statement: Prove the following for all large $x$: there is a choice of congruence classes $a_p$ for all primes $p\leq x$ and a decomposition $\{p\leq x\}=A\sqcup B$ into two non-empty sets such that, for all $n<x$, there exist some $p\in A$ and $q\in B$ such that $n\equiv a_p\pmod{p}$ and $n\equiv a_q\pmod{q}$.

nodes.label: Is there a polynomial $f:\mathbb{Z}\to \mathbb{Z}$ of degree at least $2$ and a set $A\subset \mathbb{Z}$ such that for any $n\in \mathbb{Z}
nodes.statement: Is there a polynomial $f:\mathbb{Z}\to \mathbb{Z}$ of degree at least $2$ and a set $A\subset \mathbb{Z}$ such that for any $n\in \mathbb{Z}$ there is exactly one $a\in A$ and $b\in \{ f(k) : k\in\mathbb{Z}\}$ such that $n=a+b$?
nodes.formal_statement:
theorem erdos_477 : answer(sorry) ↔
    ∃ f : ℤ[X], 2 ≤ f.degree ∧ ∃ A : Set ℤ,
      ∀ z, ∃! ab ∈ A ×ˢ (f.eval '' {n | 0 < n}), z = ab.1 + ab.2
nodes.closure_condition: The missing input is an obstruction lemma for degree >= 3 that replaces the quadratic difference-set argument. Concretely: prove that for every polynomial f in Z[X] of degree >= 3 (smallest open case f = X^3), no set A subset Z can be a perfect additive complement of the value set {f(n)} — i.e. Z cannot be tiled exactly-once by A (+) f(Z). The degree-2 case is SOLVED because f(Z)-f(Z) contains every multiple of a fixed q (q = 2b if b != 0, q = 4a if b = 0), so any (necessarily infinite) A has A-A hitting a multiple of q, yielding a forced double representation n = a+f(k) = b+f(-k). For deg >= 3 the difference set f(Z)-f(Z) is sparse and is NOT a union of full arithmetic progressions, so it contains the multiples of no fixed modulus; the pigeonhole on A-A collapses. The precise bottleneck object is therefore: a structural impossibility certificate for exact additive tilings of Z by a sparse polynomial value set of degree >= 3 — e.g. a counting/density contradiction (the value set {f(n) : |n| <= N} has size ~N while a perfect complement would need ~ X/N elements of A in [0,X], and exact-once representation must hold for ALL n, a condition the sparse non-AP difference structure should preclude). Equivalently, prove the cubic (X^3) variant erdos_477.variants.X_pow_three. There is no candidate construction believed to exist (owner and Erdős/Graham expect the answer NO), so closure = a proof of universal non-tileability for deg >= 3, not a search for an example.

For any finite sequence of $n$ (not necessarily distinct) integers $A=(x_1,\ldots,x_n)$ let $T(A)$ denote the sequence of length $rn$ given by\[(a_ix_j+b_i)_{1\leq j\leq n, 1\leq i\leq r}.\]Prove that, if $A_1=(1)$ and $A_{i+1}=T(A_i)$, then there must be some $A_k$ with repeated elements.
